full_text_rationale: Full text describes self-reflection converted into semantic experience summaries stored in long-term episodic memory and reused in subsequent trials, with HumanEval coding evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 003; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Noah Shinn, Federico Cassano, Edward Berman, Ashwin Gopinath, Karthik Narasimhan, Shunyu Yao
titulo: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
formato_armazenamento: Reflective natural-language experience summaries stored in a long-term episodic memory buffer and injected into later trials; trajectory history acts as short-term memory.
framework_agente: Custom Reflexion agent framework
llm_utilizado: GPT-4 for code-generation experiments; GPT variants for decision-making and reasoning experiments
benchmarks_utilizados: AlfWorld, HotPotQA, HumanEval, MBPP, LeetcodeHardGym
metricas_desempenho: Success rate; pass@1 accuracy; test-generation accuracy; ablation accuracy
baseline_comparado: Baseline agents without Reflexion; GPT-4 single-sample code generation; CoT / episodic-memory ablations; previous SOTA systems such as CodeT and CodeRL
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Sliding-window memory in experiments: maximum 1 experience for code generation and 3 experiences for AlfWorld.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: Reflexion stores verbal feedback in episodic memory and improves over baseline agents across decision-making, reasoning, and programming tasks. On code generation, Reflexion reports 91.0% pass@1 on HumanEval Python, 68.0% on HumanEval Rust, 77.1% on MBPP Python, 75.4% on MBPP Rust, and 15.0% on LeetcodeHard Python.
desempenho_vs_baseline: HumanEval Python improves from GPT-4 80.1% pass@1 to 91.0% (+10.9pp); HumanEval Rust improves from GPT-4 60.0% to 68.0% (+8.0pp); LeetcodeHard Python improves from GPT-4 7.5% to 15.0% (+7.5pp).
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Depends on self-evaluation/test-generation quality; long-term memory is capped in experiments; no formal guarantee of improvement; autonomous code execution requires isolation.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on specific LLM capability and generated tests; some benchmarks/tasks are outside software-engineering workflows; memory size and feedback policies are manually configured.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes STOP recursively self-improving its own code-generation improver across iterations, preserving improved improver code and showing transfer to new downstream algorithmic/code-generation tasks with quantitative results.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 003; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Eric Zelikman, Eliana Lorch, Lester Mackey, Adam Kalai
titulo: Self-Taught Optimizer (STOP): Recursively Self-Improving Code Generation
formato_armazenamento: Successive versions of an executable language-model scaffolding improver program; the improved improver code is retained and reused to optimize downstream code-generation tasks.
framework_agente: STOP self-improving code-generation improver
benchmarks_utilizados: Learning parity with noise; string grid distance; modular quadratic assignment; Maxcut; parity without noise; sandbox-bypass probe
metricas_desempenho: Meta-utility on downstream tasks; transfer utility; unsandboxing rate
baseline_comparado: Seed improver I0; chain-of-thought baseline; greedy iterative improver; GPT-3.5 and Mixtral comparisons
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Improver code evolves over recursive iterations; number of retained iterations depends on configured recursion depth T.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: With GPT-4, STOP improves downstream task performance over 1-3 self-improvement rounds and transfers to new tasks. Table 1 reports improvements from seed to improved improver: string grid distance 44.3% to 56.7%, modular quadratic assignment 20.6% to 22.1%, Maxcut 58.7% to 74.2%, and parity without noise 59.3% to 81.7%.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Improved improvers outperform the seed improver on all transfer tasks reported in Table 1; on LPN, the paper reports consistent mean downstream performance improvement with GPT-4.
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Not full recursive self-improvement because LM weights are unchanged; improvements are stochastic and can be non-monotonic; weaker models can degrade; generated code sometimes attempts to bypass sandbox restrictions.
ameacas_validade: Small downstream task set; synthetic algorithmic utilities; results depend on model version and utility design; sandbox detection uses syntactic checks.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes AgileGen maintaining a self-updating memory pool of prior user decision-making scenarios, retrieving similar entries for future software-generation tasks, and evaluating generated software with CodeBLEU, Pass@1, human evaluation, time, cost, and ablation-style comparisons.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 003; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Sai Zhang, Zhenchang Xing, Ronghui Guo, Fangzhou Xu, Lei Chen, Zhaoyuan Zhang, Xiaowang Zhang, Zhiyong Feng, Zhiqiang Zhuang
titulo: Empowering Agile-Based Generative Software Development through Human-AI Teamwork
formato_armazenamento: Memory pool of prior user decision-making scenarios and requirements/scenario entries, populated after user scenario decisions and reused as human experiential knowledge for similar new requirements.
framework_agente: AgileGen
benchmarks_utilizados: 50projects50days; SRDD; 30 website-application cases; 10 real user-created projects; 20 sampled SRDD projects
metricas_desempenho: CodeBLEU, Pass@1, execution time, Pro_Code, cost, Code Executability, User Experience Questionnaire
baseline_comparado: AutoGPT, ChatDev_23, ChatDev_24, GPT-Engineer, GPT3.5+CoT, GPT4+CoT, GPT4o+CoT, ablations
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Memory pool starts empty and grows as users make scenario decisions; no bounded-size policy reported.
custo_por_tarefa: AgileGen GPT3.5 average cost $0.0110; AgileGen GPT4 average cost $0.1490 on 50projects50days execution-performance comparison.
resultado_principal: AgileGen uses Gherkin-centered agile generation plus a memory pool of user decision scenarios. On 50projects50days, AgileGen GPT3.5 reports CodeBLEU 0.339 and Pass@1 62.5%; AgileGen GPT4 reports CodeBLEU 0.362 and Pass@1 71.5%.
desempenho_vs_baseline: AgileGen GPT3.5 outperforms ChatDev_24 GPT3.5 by +0.089 CodeBLEU and +16.4pp Pass@1; AgileGen GPT4 outperforms GPT4o+CoT by +0.105 CodeBLEU and +24.7pp Pass@1.
custo_vs_baseline: AgileGen GPT3.5 reports $0.0110 average cost, lower than AutoGPT GPT3.5 ($0.0442), ChatDev_23 ($0.0259), and ChatDev_24 ($0.0192), but higher than GPT3.5+CoT ($0.0028).
limitacoes: Human-in-the-loop workflow; memory-pool contribution is embedded in a larger AgileGen system; no isolated memory-only causal estimate in the extracted results.
ameacas_validade: Dataset/task selection, generated tests, user-evaluation subjectivity, and LLM version differences may affect generalization.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes FALCON as a coding-optimization system with explicit long-term and short-term memory buffers that store historical tasks, errors, and feedback for later code-generation improvement with empirical evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 003; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Zeyuan Li, Yangfan He, Lewei He, Jianhui Wang, Tianyu Shi, Bin Lei, Yuchen Li, Qiuwu Chen
titulo: FALCON: Feedback-driven Adaptive Long/short-term memory reinforced Coding OptimizatioN
formato_armazenamento: Long-term memory buffer stores task descriptions, generated code/solutions, test/compiler feedback, and evaluation scores; short-term memory stores recent samples, error lines, compiler feedback, and immediate feedback for reward allocation.
framework_agente: FALCON meta-reinforcement learning framework
benchmarks_utilizados: APPS, HumanEval, MBPP, CODAL-Bench
metricas_desempenho: pass@1, pass@5, coding-preference metrics
baseline_comparado: CodeT5 base, PPOCoder, CodeRL, RLTF, DeepSeek-Coder-Instruct without FALCON
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Long-term memory accumulates historical tasks and feedback; short-term memory captures current/recent feedback. No storage bound reported.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: FALCON combines long/short-term memory with meta-reinforcement learning for code generation. On APPS with CodeT5 770M, it reports pass@1 all 3.50 and pass@5 all 8.17, outperforming RLTF (3.27 / 7.87). On HumanEval/MBPP, it reports 82.9 and 80.7 pass@1.
desempenho_vs_baseline: HumanEval improves from RLTF 76.8 to 82.9 (+6.1pp); MBPP improves from RLTF 75.9 to 80.7 (+4.8pp); APPS all pass@1 improves from RLTF 3.27 to 3.50 (+0.23pp).
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Preprint-style reporting; no cost data; memory and reward components are evaluated inside a broader fine-tuning/RL setup.
ameacas_validade: Benchmark-specific tuning, base-model differences, and unclear public-code permanence may affect reproducibility; CODAL-Bench preference outcomes are not directly comparable to functional pass rates.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes Iterative Experience Refinement for software-developing agents, accumulating and refining experiences across task batches and evaluating software-development task performance.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `qian2024iterative` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_experience_bank. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Chen Qian, Jiahao Li, Yufan Dang, Wei Liu, YiFei Wang, Zihao Xie, Weize Chen, Cheng Yang, Yingli Zhang, Zhiyuan Liu, Maosong Sun
titulo: Iterative Experience Refinement of Software-Developing Agents
formato_armazenamento: Pool de experiências shortcut como pares chave-valor: solution-to-instruction (conhecimento do agente instructivo) e instruction-to-solution (conhecimento do agente responsivo). Shortcuts extraídos de nós não-adjacentes na cadeia de execução. Embeddings via text-embedding-ada-002 para recuperação vetorial.
framework_agente: custom (estende ChatDev)
benchmarks_utilizados: SRDD dataset (1200 descrições de requisitos de software, 40 categorias, dividido em 6 batches)
metricas_desempenho: Completeness (α), Executability (β), Consistency (γ), Quality (α×β×γ), Duration
baseline_comparado: GPTEngineer, MetaGPT, ChatDev, ECL (Experiential Co-Learning)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Cresce com batches; eliminação heurística reduz para 11.54% do pool original (930 de 8053 experiências) mantendo desempenho superior
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: IER melhora Quality de 0.4665 (ChatDev) para 0.6372 (IER-Successive), +36.6% relativo. Padrão cumulativo oferece estabilidade maior que successive; eliminação de experiências atinge desempenho superior com apenas 11.54% do pool original.
desempenho_vs_baseline: +36.6% Quality vs ChatDev (0.6372 vs 0.4665); +10.3% Quality vs ECL (0.6372 vs 0.5775); +11% Executability vs ECL (0.9146 vs 0.8643)
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Avaliação apenas com ChatGPT-3.5; métricas customizadas podem não refletir qualidade real de software; SRDD limitado a geração de software simples; sem avaliação em benchmarks padrão (SWE-bench); duração aumenta com IER (~180s vs ~148s ChatDev)
ameacas_validade: ChatGPT-3.5 como único modelo; Completeness/Executability/Consistency são proxies automatizados sem validação humana; SRDD gera software simples não representativo de projetos reais; embeddings ada-002 podem não capturar similaridade semântica de código adequadamente

full_text_rationale: Full text describes EvoCoder accumulating prior issue-reproduction trajectories into common and repository-specific experience pools that are updated and reused on later software issues, with SWE-bench evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 003; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Yalan Lin, Yingwei Ma, Rongyu Cao, Binhua Li, Fei Huang, Xiaodong Gu, Yongbin Li
titulo: LLMs as Continuous Learners: Improving the Reproduction of Defective Code in Software Issues
formato_armazenamento: Hierarchical experience pool with a general/common pool and repository-specific pools. Experiences are distilled from issue-reproduction trajectories by a Reflection LLM and can be added, modified, or removed.
framework_agente: EvoCoder, based on SWE-Agent/CodeR-style issue reproduction interaction
llm_utilizado: NR for actor/reflection model; GPT-4 used for LLM-as-judge evaluation
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Lite issue reproduction; issue-resolving evaluation with AutoCodeRover and Agentless
metricas_desempenho: LLM-judged and human-judged reproduction accuracy; number of resolved issues; fix rate; error-transition analysis
baseline_comparado: CodeR, LIBRO, AutoCodeRover, Agentless, and versions with EvoCoder reproduction code
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Experience pool is dynamically updated with add/modify/remove operations to avoid experience bloating; no numeric size bound reported.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: EvoCoder improves issue-code reproduction by maintaining common and repository-specific experiences. Table 1 reports EvoCoder at 54.00% LLM-judged and 53.33% human-judged reproduction accuracy. Applying EvoCoder to debuggers increases resolved issues for AutoCodeRover from 15 to 18 and Agentless from 16 to 18.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Compared with CodeR, reproduction accuracy improves from 34.00% to 54.00% LLM-judged (+20.00pp) and from 33.33% to 53.33% human-judged (+20.00pp). AutoCodeRover fix rate improves from 22.06% to 26.47%; Agentless from 23.53% to 26.47%.
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Focuses on issue reproduction rather than full patch generation; actor/reflection LLM model details are not explicit in extracted text; unresolved cases remain due to model limitations and ambiguous issue descriptions.
ameacas_validade: LLM-as-judge and human judging may introduce evaluation subjectivity; SWE-bench Lite subset and reproduction-task framing may not generalize to all issue-resolution workflows.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes SAIL extracting and archiving UI-testing skills in a memory/knowledge base from source test cases and reusing them for later target-app test generation, with evaluation against baselines.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 003; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Mengzhou Wu, Hao Wang, Jun Ren, Yuan Cao, Yuetong Li, Alex Jiang, Dezhi Ran, Yitao Hu, Wei Yang, Tao Xie
titulo: Skill-Adpative Imitation Learning for UI Test Reuse
formato_armazenamento: Knowledge base/archive of multi-level abstracted UI testing skills extracted from source test cases, then selectively reused for target-app test generation with context- and history-aware adaptation.
framework_agente: SAIL skill-adaptive imitation learning framework
benchmarks_utilizados: 104 UI test-migration cases from 35 popular Android apps; SemFinder dataset / CraftDroid-related evaluation subjects
metricas_desempenho: Success rate; Top-1 event-matching accuracy; ablation success rates
baseline_comparado: CraftDroid, SemFinder, CraftDroid-SemFinder, CraftDroid-DeepSeek-V2, CraftDroid-GPT-4o-mini, TraceDroid, TargetDroid
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Source-test skills are archived as a knowledge base for later target-app generation; no growth bound reported.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: SAIL extracts source-test skills and reuses them for UI test migration. Table 3 reports SAIL-DeepSeek-V2 success rate 63.6%, SAIL-GPT-4o 62.9%, and SAIL-GPT-4o-mini 58.4%.
desempenho_vs_baseline: SAIL-DeepSeek-V2 improves over CraftDroid 25.5% to 63.6% overall (+38.1pp; reported as 149% higher SR). In non-1-to-1 mappings, SAIL-GPT-4o reaches 55.6% versus CraftDroid 14.1% (reported as 294% improvement).
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Focuses on Android UI test migration; no cost/token data; source-code availability not explicit in extracted text; success depends on LLM and UI-state representations.
ameacas_validade: Dataset composition, time limits for baselines, prompt engineering, and app/UI heterogeneity may affect external validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes a self-improving coding agent that keeps an archive of previous agents and benchmark results, selects the best archived agent for the next self-modification iteration, and reports gains on SWE-Bench Verified, LiveCodeBench, and synthetic benchmarks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 004; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Maxime Robeyns, Martin Szummer, Laurence Aitchison
titulo: A Self-Improving Coding Agent
formato_armazenamento: Archive of previous self-modified coding-agent implementations, benchmark results, average cost/time/token metrics, and archive-analysis traces; the best archived agent is selected as the next meta-agent for self-modification.
framework_agente: Self-Improving Coding Agent (SICA)
llm_utilizado: Claude 3.5 Sonnet v2 for most agents; o3-mini for a reasoning agent
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-Bench Verified subset, LiveCodeBench, synthetic file-editing benchmark, synthetic symbol-location benchmark
metricas_desempenho: Benchmark accuracy; average per-problem cost; average per-problem time; average total tokens; cached-token percentage; utility combining score, cost, and time
baseline_comparado: Initial archived agent at iteration 0 and later self-improved agent iterations; paper also compares against ADAS conceptually.
tokens_entrada_por_tarefa: NR; Table 1 reports average total tokens per benchmark problem from 0.22M to 0.30M, not split into input/output.
tokens_saida_por_tarefa: NR; token totals are not split into input/output.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Archive retains previous agent iterations and benchmark records across 15 self-improvement iterations in the reported run.
custo_por_tarefa: Average per-problem cost ranges from $1.58 to $2.70 across iterations; the 15-iteration API run cost approximately $7,000.
reducao_tokens: NR; cached-token percentage is reported but no explicit token-reduction baseline is given.
resultado_principal: SICA self-edits its own agent code and improves on coding benchmarks. Table 1 reports SWE-Bench Verified accuracy increasing from 0.17 at iteration 0 to a peak of 0.53 at iteration 14, with LiveCodeBench improving from 0.65 to 0.71 by iteration 15.
desempenho_vs_baseline: SWE-Bench Verified improves from 17% to 53% at the best reported iteration (+36pp). File editing improves from 0.82 to 0.96 at peak, symbol location from 0.35 to 0.43 at peak, and LiveCodeBench from 0.65 to 0.71 by iteration 15.
custo_vs_baseline: Cost and time are included in the utility, but the paper does not claim a clean cost-reduction result versus a fixed baseline; average per-problem cost at iteration 0 is $1.91 and at iteration 15 is $1.70.
limitacoes: Novel self-modification ideas are hard to generate; poor early ideas can create path dependence; benchmark timeouts may depress initial scores; much improvement comes from faster editing and cost reductions rather than only higher SWE-Bench capability; safety requires observability and evaluation gates.
ameacas_validade: Random benchmark subsets, short timeouts, cost caps, model/provider choice, and the fixed benchmark set affect external validity; no model-weight updates are studied.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes AccelOpt maintaining and updating optimization memory from prior slow-fast kernel pairs and exploration experiences, with NKIBench evaluation for AI accelerator kernel optimization.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `zhang2025accelopt` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_experience_bank. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Genghan Zhang, Shaowei Zhu, Anjiang Wei, Zhenyu Song, Allen Nie, Zhen Jia, Nandita Vijaykumar, Yida Wang, Kunle Olukotun
titulo: AccelOpt: A Self-Improving LLM Agentic System for AI Accelerator Kernel Optimization
formato_armazenamento: Fila de experience items com capacidade fixa (ExpN): cada item contém par slow-fast kernel (pseudocódigo dos segmentos otimizados) + estratégia de otimização generalizada em NL gerada pelo summarizer. Itens positivos (speedup > t_pos) e negativos (slowdown > 1/t_neg) curados separadamente.
framework_agente: custom
benchmarks_utilizados: NKIBench (14 kernels de workloads LLM reais em AWS Trainium 1 e 2)
metricas_desempenho: Percentage of peak throughput, Fast@p, geometric mean speedup, correctness rate
baseline_comparado: Claude Sonnet 4 (repeated sampling), kernels iniciais, beam search only (sem memória)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Fila com capacidade fixa (ExpN=16); entradas mais antigas descartadas ao atingir limite; crescimento sublinear controlado por TopK
custo_por_tarefa: $96.10-$223.23 por configuração completa (14 kernels, variando por executor/ExpN)
resultado_principal: AccelOpt melhora throughput médio de 49% para 61% do pico no Trainium 1 e de 45% para 59% no Trainium 2, igualando Claude Sonnet 4 com custo 26x menor usando modelos open-source.
desempenho_vs_baseline: +12pp throughput médio no Trainium 1 (49%→61%); +14pp no Trainium 2 (45%→59%) vs kernels iniciais; equivalente a Claude Sonnet 4
custo_vs_baseline: 26x mais barato que Claude Sonnet 4 com desempenho equivalente ($139 AccelOpt vs ~$1733 Claude Sonnet 4); memória economiza 16-17% do custo vs beam search puro
limitacoes: Avaliação limitada a kernels NKI para AWS Trainium; LLMs não conseguem otimizar certos kernels próximos ao pico ou com espaço de otimização difícil; dependência de serviço de profiling distribuído; apenas modelos open-source testados como base
ameacas_validade: NKIBench contém apenas 14 kernels; Trainium é acelerador específico, resultados podem não generalizar para GPUs/TPUs; modelos open-source específicos testados; custo medido por preço de API que pode variar

full_text_rationale: Full text describes Agent KB as a shared cross-framework knowledge base that distills heterogeneous agent trajectories into reusable experience units and evaluates cross-domain transfer including SWE-bench.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 004; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Xiangru Tang, Tianrui Qin, Tianhao Peng, Ziyang Zhou, Yanjun Shao, Tingting Du, Xinming Wei, Peng Xia, Fang Wu, He Zhu, Ge Zhang, Jiaheng Liu, Xingyao Wang, Sirui Hong, Chenglin Wu, Hao Cheng, Chi Wang, Wangchunshu Zhou
titulo: Agent KB: Leveraging Cross-Domain Experience for Agentic Problem Solving
formato_armazenamento: Framework-agnostic structured experience units E=<task embedding, structured goal constraints, action-reasoning pairs, metadata> stored behind REST APIs, with addition, deduplication, conflict resolution, and adaptive utility-based eviction.
framework_agente: AGENT KB integrated with smolagents, OpenHands, OWL, SWE-Agent, and other agent stacks
llm_utilizado: GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, Claude-3.7, Qwen-3 32B, DeepSeek-R1, o3-mini
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Lite, GAIA, Humanity's Last Exam Bio/Chem, GPQA
metricas_desempenho: Pass@1/pass@2/pass@3 accuracy; success rate; retrieval latency; memory footprint; token and monetary cost budgets; ablation accuracy
baseline_comparado: Baseline agent frameworks without AGENT KB, AGENT KB pass@1/pass@2/pass@3 variants, ablations without hybrid retrieval and feedback components, manually curated knowledge comparison.
tokens_entrada_por_tarefa: GAIA evaluation averages 212,586 prompt tokens per task including action loop/log summary/planning/feedback; SWE-bench hinting averages 4,184 prompt tokens per issue.
tokens_saida_por_tarefa: GAIA evaluation averages 42,221 completion tokens per task; SWE-bench hinting averages 530 completion tokens per issue.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Knowledge base grows as heterogeneous frameworks contribute execution experiences; appendix reports thousand-entry source collections and adaptive eviction under memory pressure.
custo_por_tarefa: GAIA evaluation averages $0.52 per task, with retrieval loop adding about $0.27 over the full validation run; SWE-bench hinting averages $0.0037 per issue.
reducao_tokens: NR; paper reports low marginal hint cost and short prompts but not token reduction versus baseline.
resultado_principal: AGENT KB enables cross-framework experience sharing and improves multiple agent/model combinations. On SWE-bench Lite, GPT-4.1 with SWE-Agent improves from 24.3% to 38.0% at max-iteration 50 and 42.3% at max-iteration 100 by pass@3; OpenHands with Claude-3.7 improves from 30.0% to 51.0% at max-iteration 50.
desempenho_vs_baseline: SWE-Agent GPT-4.1 improves 24.3% to 38.0% at 50 iterations (+13.7pp) and 27.0% to 42.3% at 100 iterations (+15.3pp). OpenHands Claude-3.7 improves 30.0% to 51.0% at 50 iterations (+21.0pp) and 41.3% to 53.3% at 100 iterations (+12.0pp).
custo_vs_baseline: AGENT KB retrieval overhead is reported as negligible: less than 0.4% of GAIA run cost and average SWE-bench hinting cost below $0.004 per issue.
limitacoes: External experiences can cause knowledge interference; the design needs disagreement gating, conflict resolution, and adaptive eviction; results depend on source-experience quality and stronger-to-weaker transfer.
ameacas_validade: Cross-framework compatibility, source benchmark selection, generated-experience abstraction quality, model mix, and budget caps may affect transferability.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes AlphaOPT, a self-improving solver-verified experience library that stores structured modeling and code-implementation insights, retrieves and refines them across optimization-program instances, and evaluates performance on multiple optimization/program-formulation benchmarks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 004; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Minwei Kong, Ao Qu, Xiaotong Guo, Wenbin Ouyang, Chonghe Jiang, Han Zheng, Yining Ma, Dingyi Zhuang, Yuhan Tang, Junyi Li, Shenhao Wang, Haris Koutsopoulos, Hai Wang, Cathy Wu, Jinhua Zhao
titulo: AlphaOPT: Formulating Optimization Programs with Self-Improving LLM Experience Library
formato_armazenamento: Self-improving experience library of solver-verified modeling and code-implementation insights represented as taxonomy, condition, explanation, and example tuples, organized in a dynamic hierarchical taxonomy.
framework_agente: AlphaOPT
llm_utilizado: GPT-4o with temperature 0 unless otherwise specified
benchmarks_utilizados: NLP4LP, NL4OPT, IndustryOR, MAMO/ComplexLP, LogiOR, OptiBench
metricas_desempenho: Accuracy/success rate; micro-averaged accuracy; macro-averaged accuracy; ablation accuracy; training-size growth curve
baseline_comparado: Standard prompting, OptiMus, ORThought, ORLM, LLMOPT, Reflexion, ExpeL, AlphaOPT ablations without refinement and without insight examples.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Library grows from training tasks but uses online merging and refinement to bound redundant insights; training-size analysis reports 100, 200, and 300 training items.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: AlphaOPT outperforms prompt-based, fine-tuning, and experience-learning baselines across optimization-program formulation benchmarks. Table 1 reports AlphaOPT micro/macro averages of 80.1/76.4 on test splits and 86.5/74.8 on out-of-distribution datasets.
desempenho_vs_baseline: AlphaOPT exceeds ExpeL on test micro/macro averages (80.1/76.4 vs 69.9/66.3) and out-of-distribution micro/macro averages (86.5/74.8 vs 74.3/64.1). Removing refinement reduces out-of-distribution macro from 74.8 to 70.5.
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Focused on optimization modeling and solver-backed code; relies on solver verification and task ground-truth objectives; no token or monetary cost reporting; theoretical convergence argument is presented as a principled sketch rather than a strict theorem.
ameacas_validade: Dataset overlap in competing fine-tuning baselines, stochasticity in LLM outputs, task-family distribution, and solver-specific verification rules may affect external validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes SATLUTION as a repository-scale LLM-based code-evolution framework that persists evolved solver repositories, self-evolves evolution policies/rules across cycles, and reports PAR-2 and solved-instance results against SAT Competition benchmarks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 004; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Cunxi Yu, Rongjian Liang, Chia-Tung Ho, Haoxing Ren
titulo: Autonomous Code Evolution Meets NP-Completeness
formato_armazenamento: Evolved SAT solver repositories plus a rulebase of initialization rules, programming constraints, pre-evaluation checks, post-evaluation analysis, champion rules, forbidden patterns, and failure rules maintained across evolution cycles.
framework_agente: SATLUTION built on Cursor with planning and coding Claude agents
llm_utilizado: Two Claude models in Cursor for planning and coding
benchmarks_utilizados: SAT Competition 2024 and SAT Competition 2025 benchmark instances
metricas_desempenho: PAR-2 score; number of solved instances; runtime/cactus plots; SAT and UNSAT category results; correctness validation outcomes
baseline_comparado: Human-designed SAT Competition 2024 solvers; SAT Competition 2025 winner and runner-up; AlphaEvolve comparison at capability level.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Dozens of evolution cycles over full solver repositories; cumulative code modifications and rules are retained across cycles.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
reducao_tokens: NR; paper states Cursor token caching/file indexing helped avoid prohibitive token costs but gives no numeric token reduction.
resultado_principal: SATLUTION evolves repository-scale SAT solvers from SAT Competition 2024 codebases and reports lower PAR-2 scores and more solved instances than the top SAT Competition 2025 solvers, despite training feedback coming from 2024 benchmarks.
desempenho_vs_baseline: On the SAT Competition 2025 comparison, the top evolved solvers solve 347, 345, and 344 instances, compared with 334 and 331 instances for the gold and silver 2025 winners; figures report lowest PAR-2 scores on 2025 and 2024 benchmarks.
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Fully automated operation struggled; the effective setup used targeted human intervention for high-level direction; agents were prone to SAT/UNSAT correctness failures and memory errors such as segmentation faults; domain-specific idea generation remained weak.
ameacas_validade: Hardware/evaluator differences versus official SAT Competition runs, domain-specific SAT benchmarks, human steering in later stages, and repository-scale verification choices affect reproducibility and generalization.

contextual_role: Assessment-framework evidence for memory-pillar metrics in deployed agentic CloudOps systems.
full_text_rationale: Full text proposes an Agent Assessment Framework with explicit memory-pillar metrics for storage/update correctness, retrieval accuracy, latency, and coverage, and validates it on a representative Autonomous CloudOps agentic system in a software-engineering/operations setting.
extractor_notes: Contextual assessment framework; validates memory metrics but does not propose a persistence architecture.
autores: Sreemaee Akshathala, Bassam Adnan, Mahisha Ramesh, Karthik Vaidhyanathan, Basil Muhammed, Kannan Parthasarathy
titulo: Beyond Task Completion: An Assessment Framework for Evaluating Agentic AI Systems
formato_armazenamento: NA
framework_agente: MOYA / representative Autonomous CloudOps multi-agent system
llm_utilizado: GPT-4o for model selection/evaluation; text-embedding-3-small for ChromaDB memory embeddings; Sonnet 4.5 for synthetic CloudOps data generation
benchmarks_utilizados: Three Autonomous CloudOps scenarios: cost optimization, security incident response, and multi-agent root cause analysis.
metricas_desempenho: Task completion, tool usage, tool sequence, expected calls, policy adherence, dependency inquiry, memory precision, memory recall, memory F1, judge scores, runtime and cost overhead.
baseline_comparado: Baseline task-completion/tool-usage evaluation versus four-pillar Agent Assessment Framework.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: NA
custo_por_tarefa: Reports judge-protocol overhead: LLM-as-Judge total cost $0.0593; Agent-as-Judge total cost $0.9572; average execution cost $0.0621.
resultado_principal: Framework-specific metrics reveal failures hidden by task-completion baselines. Memory F1 varied by scenario: 31.1% (S1), 18.1% (S2), and 51.32% (S3), while baseline metrics stayed coarse.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Adds memory/tool/policy diagnostics beyond baseline task completion and tool usage; e.g. S2 has 100% task completion but only 13.1% memory recall and 18.1% memory F1.
custo_vs_baseline: Adds judge/evaluation overhead; LLM-as-Judge is much cheaper than Agent-as-Judge in reported totals.
limitacoes: Validation uses three CloudOps scenarios and synthetic data from production-inspired issues; generality to coding agents beyond CloudOps remains untested.
ameacas_validade: Uniform temperature/model choices, LLM-as-judge bias, synthetic scenario construction, and small scenario count may affect generalization.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes CodeDistiller generating a vetted reusable code-example library from repositories for scientific coding agents, with automatic/domain-expert evaluation and downstream agent evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 004; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Peter A. Jansen, Samiah Hassan, Pragnya Narasimha
titulo: CodeDistiller: Automatically Generating Code Libraries for Scientific Coding Agents
formato_armazenamento: Vetted library of executable, debugged scientific code examples distilled from GitHub repositories, including example code, requirements, suitability descriptions, and evidence of successful execution.
framework_agente: CodeDistiller and downstream Code-RAG / CodeScientist-style automated scientific discovery agent
llm_utilizado: GPT-OSS-120B, GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4.5, smaller classifier variants such as GPT-5-mini and Claude Haiku 4.5
benchmarks_utilizados: 250 materials-science GitHub repositories; 50-repository domain-expert validation subset; 60 downstream materials-science tasks from 12 repositories
metricas_desempenho: Automatic successful completion; manual code-execution success; manual repository-functionality and correct-functionality judgments; runtime; debug iterations; API cost; A/B preference proportions for accuracy, completeness, and scientific soundness
baseline_comparado: Different CodeDistiller base models; downstream CodeScientist with generic materials-science examples versus CodeDistiller-generated examples.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Library is generated from a collection of repository examples; growth bound or update policy is not reported.
custo_por_tarefa: Successful-case average cost: GPT-OSS-120B $0.09, GPT-5 $0.70, Claude 4.5 $1.71; downstream runs allow up to $5 of LLM-associated costs per run.
resultado_principal: CodeDistiller converts scientific GitHub repositories into reusable code examples. Domain-expert validation reports correct functionality for 25.9% with GPT-OSS-120B, 60.5% with GPT-5, and 74.1% with Claude 4.5; downstream A/B tests prefer CodeDistiller-augmented outputs in nearly half of cases across accuracy, completeness, and soundness.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Downstream A/B preference proportions favor CodeDistiller over the generic-example baseline for accuracy (0.50 vs 0.26), completeness (0.46 vs 0.16), and soundness (0.50 vs 0.30), with ties making up the remainder.
custo_vs_baseline: Claude-generated examples cost 19 times more on average than GPT-OSS examples; no downstream cost reduction claim is reported.
limitacoes: LLM-as-judge overestimates success, especially for GPT-OSS-120B; unsuccessful examples cost more because debugging reaches hard iteration limits; domain-expert evaluation is limited to a subsample and downstream tasks are materials-science specific.
ameacas_validade: Materials-science domain focus, repository filtering, domain-expert subsampling, judge-model preference evaluation, run budget, and debug-iteration caps may affect external validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes Confucius Code Agent with a persistent note-taking and long-term memory system for cross-session continual learning, evaluated on SWE-Bench-Pro, SWE-Bench Verified, and PyTorch-Bench.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `wong2025confucius` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_persistent_workspace_state. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Sherman Wong, Zhenting Qi, Zhaodong Wang, Nathan Hu, Samuel Lin, Jun Ge, Erwin Gao, Wenlin Chen, Yilun Du, Minlan Yu, Ying Zhang
titulo: Confucius Code Agent: Scalable Agent Scaffolding for Real-World Codebases
formato_armazenamento: Hierarchical working memory (árvore de arquivos Markdown com tags de metadados e escopos de visibilidade: session, entry, runnable) + notas persistentes cross-session (Markdown em diretório por sessão, e.g. research/findings.md, solutions/bug_fix.md, incluindo hindsight notes para falhas) + context compression summaries (plano estruturado com goals, decisions, TODOs, gerado pelo Architect Agent)
framework_agente: custom (Confucius SDK)
llm_utilizado: Claude 4 Sonnet, Claude 4.5 Sonnet, Claude 4.5 Opus, GPT-5.2
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-Bench-Pro (731 tasks), SWE-Bench-Verified (500 tasks), PyTorch-Bench (custom)
metricas_desempenho: Resolve Rate (Pass@1)
baseline_comparado: SWE-Agent, Live-SWE-Agent, OpenHands, Anthropic (proprietário), OpenAI (proprietário)
tokens_entrada_por_tarefa: ~104k tokens (Run 1 sem notas); ~93k tokens (Run 2 com notas)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Working memory cresce durante sessão e é comprimida periodicamente pelo Architect Agent; notas persistentes acumulam entre sessões
custo_por_tarefa: NR
reducao_tokens: -11k tokens/tarefa (104k→93k) com notas persistentes da Run 1 aplicadas na Run 2
resultado_principal: CCA atinge 59.0% Resolve@1 no SWE-Bench-Pro com GPT-5.2, superando OpenAI (56.0%); com Claude 4.5 Sonnet atinge 52.7% vs Live-SWE-Agent 45.8%. Notas persistentes reduzem turnos de 64 para 61 e tokens de 104k para 93k, com ganho de +1.4pp em Resolve Rate.
desempenho_vs_baseline: +3.0pp vs OpenAI (59.0% vs 56.0%) com GPT-5.2; +6.9pp vs Live-SWE-Agent (52.7% vs 45.8%) com Claude 4.5 Sonnet; +1.8pp vs OpenHands (74.6% vs 72.8%) no SWE-Bench-Verified
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: PyTorch-Bench não diretamente comparável com SWE-Bench-Pro por diferenças de ambiente de execução; avaliação de notas limitada a 151 instâncias; meta-agent co-desenvolvido com benchmarks de avaliação; SDK não totalmente open-source
ameacas_validade: SWE-Bench-Verified sensível ao thinking budget do modelo; comparação com sistemas proprietários (Anthropic, OpenAI) pode ter diferenças não controladas de infraestrutura; meta-agent refinado nos mesmos benchmarks de avaliação pode levar a overfitting

full_text_rationale: Full text describes the Darwin Godel Machine maintaining an archive of generated coding agents, selecting archived agents for self-modification, preserving auditable lineages of code changes/evaluations, and improving on SWE-bench and Polyglot with baselines for self-improvement and archive/open-ended exploration.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 004; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Jenny Zhang, Shengran Hu, Cong Lu, Robert Lange, Jeff Clune
titulo: Darwin Godel Machine: Open-Ended Evolution of Self-Improving Agents
formato_armazenamento: Growing archive/tree of generated coding-agent codebases, benchmark evaluations, and auditable lineages; archived agents can be selected as parents for self-modification.
framework_agente: Darwin Godel Machine (DGM)
llm_utilizado: Claude 3.5 Sonnet (New) for self-modification and SWE-bench evaluation; o3-mini for Polyglot evaluation; transfer experiments with Claude 3.7 are discussed.
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified, Polyglot
metricas_desempenho: Solved-task percentage / pass@1; best-agent score over iterations; cost estimate; baseline comparison curves
baseline_comparado: DGM without self-improving agents, DGM without open-ended exploration, checked open-source SWE-bench SoTA, Aider on Polyglot
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Archive grows over 80 iterations, generating one new agent per iteration in the reported setup; all compilable agents are retained as potential stepping stones.
custo_por_tarefa: Estimated cost for one SWE-bench DGM run is about $22,000; each baseline SWE-bench run is about $10,000; one 60-task SWE-bench evaluation costs about $350 and one 60-task Polyglot evaluation about $5.
resultado_principal: After 80 iterations, DGM improves coding-agent performance from 20.0% to 50.0% on SWE-bench and from 14.0% to 38.0% on the Polyglot subset; full Polyglot improves from 14.2% to 30.7%.
desempenho_vs_baseline: DGM outperforms variants without self-improvement and without open-ended exploration; best discovered SWE-bench agent is comparable to checked open-source human-designed SoTA, while Polyglot improves 14.2% to 30.7% on the full benchmark.
custo_vs_baseline: DGM SWE-bench run is about $22,000 versus about $10,000 for either baseline run, so performance gains come at materially higher API cost.
limitacoes: Requires extensive compute and significant API cost; assumes coding benchmark gains reflect better self-improvement ability; archive maintenance/parent selection is fixed rather than self-modified; safety requires sandboxing, resource limits, and human oversight.
ameacas_validade: Uses staged subsets for cost control; results depend on Claude/o3-mini models, benchmark sampling, stochastic self-modification, and sandboxed execution constraints.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes ECO mining historical performance-optimizing commits into reusable anti-pattern/optimization knowledge and applying it through an LLM-driven code optimizer with production-scale evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 005; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Hannah Lin, Martin Maas, Maximilian Roquemore, Arman Hasanzadeh, Fred Lewis, Yusuf Simonson, Tzu-Wei Yang, Amir Yazdanbakhsh, Deniz Altinbuken, Florin Papa, Maggie Nolan Edmonds, Aditya Patil, Don Schwarz, Satish Chandra, Chris Kennelly, Milad Hashemi, Parthasarathy Ranganathan
titulo: ECO: An LLM-Driven Efficient Code Optimizer for Warehouse Scale Computers
formato_armazenamento: Database/dictionary of performance anti-patterns and corresponding optimizations mined from historical performance-improving commits, code review comments, issue tracker entries, and before/after source snapshots; the performance-specific fine-tuning dataset contains about 55k commits.
framework_agente: Efficient Code Optimizer (ECO) production workflow
llm_utilizado: Fine-tuned Gemini Pro 1.0 as the LLM edit generator; system is described as LLM-agnostic
benchmarks_utilizados: Google production code-review/deployment pipeline, microbenchmark edits, retrieval-model evaluation, human/automated code review, fleet-wide production profiler
metricas_desempenho: Submitted commits; changed lines of code; production success rate; normalized-core savings; MAP@K retrieval metrics; CodeBLEU and human quality scores; code-review outcomes
baseline_comparado: Human baseline for microbenchmark edits; historical production changes; retrieval baselines including bag-of-words, deep text embeddings, and deep code embeddings; production before/after profiler measurements.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Anti-pattern database grows over time as engineers mine additional historical optimization commits and curated sources.
custo_por_tarefa: NR as monetary LLM cost; deployment reports over 2M normalized cores saved, over 500k normalized cores per quarter, and fleet-wide resource savings from 6.4k landed commits.
resultado_principal: ECO mines reusable optimization patterns from historical commits and applies them through an LLM-driven optimizer at warehouse scale. The system has landed over 6.4k production commits and over 25k changed lines with >99.5% production success rate.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Production deployment reports over 2M normalized cores saved, more than 500k normalized cores saved per quarter, and successful edits across multiple anti-pattern categories; ECO scales human optimization effort beyond manual search.
custo_vs_baseline: Reports normalized-core resource savings rather than per-task API cost; no direct monetary LLM-cost baseline is provided.
limitacoes: Operated by human engineers who choose anti-patterns and intervene when needed; all code requires code review; system is evaluated in a proprietary Google production environment; no public source release.
ameacas_validade: Production-internal codebase, sanitized examples, human-review policy, continuous profiler assumptions, anti-pattern selection, and infrastructure-specific normalized-core metric may limit external replication.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes EvoLattice as a persistent multi-alternative graph representation that preserves reusable program components and historical evidence during LLM-guided program discovery, with benchmark evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 005; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Kamer Ali Yuksel
titulo: EvoLattice: Persistent Internal-Population Evolution through Multi-Alternative Quality-Diversity Graph Representations for LLM-Guided Program Discovery
formato_armazenamento: Persistent directed acyclic graph where each node stores multiple alternatives; every valid path defines an executable candidate, and alternatives retain performance statistics for reuse, recombination, mutation, and pruning.
framework_agente: EvoLattice
llm_utilizado: GPT-OSS-20B with temperature 0.0 for mutation steps; maximum output budget 65,536 tokens
benchmarks_utilizados: NAS-Bench-Suite-Zero Medium proxy-quality benchmark; program-synthesis proxy and optimizer meta-learning tasks
metricas_desempenho: Spearman rank correlation; confidence interval half-width; best-score trajectory; structural validity; stability under mutation
baseline_comparado: Spectral Energy, Covariance Trace, Spectral + Covariance fixed proxy, ZeroLM, LPZero, FunSearch-style proxy generation, AlphaEvolve-style diff evolution, ShinkaEvolve-like parent sampling.
tokens_saida_por_tarefa: Maximum output budget of 65,536 tokens per mutation generation; average output tokens not reported.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Graph grows by accumulating alternatives at nodes; pruning weak alternatives is needed to control long-run structure size.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: EvoLattice preserves internal diversity and outperforms fixed and LLM-guided single-path evolution baselines on NAS-Bench-Suite-Zero. Table 1 reports EvoLattice proxy Spearman rho 0.15-0.16 with CI half-width ±0.04, the highest among listed methods.
desempenho_vs_baseline: EvoLattice exceeds AlphaEvolve-style diff evolution (0.11 ±0.06), ShinkaEvolve-like parent sampling (0.12 ±0.05), FunSearch-style proxy generation (0.09 ±0.07), and LPZero (0.10 ±0.05).
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Combinatorial path growth creates scalability pressure; evaluating all paths becomes costly as nodes and alternatives increase; pruning introduces a tradeoff between exploration and compactness.
ameacas_validade: Training-free NAS proxy benchmark focus, synthetic/meta-learning task setup, GPT-OSS-20B configuration, and path-sampling choices may affect generalization to full agent systems.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes K3 Trans constructing and continuously evolving a translation knowledge base from target-language codebases, repository context, and prior translation results, with repository-level code-translation evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 005; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Guangsheng Ou, Mingwei Liu, Yuxuan Chen, Xueying Du, Shengbo Wang, Zekai Zhang, Xin Peng, Zibin Zheng
titulo: Evolving Triple Knowledge-Augmented LLMs for Code Translation in Repository Context
formato_armazenamento: Self-evolving triple translation knowledge base containing dependency usage examples from the repository, target-language code samples from existing projects, and successful translation function pairs from prior translation history.
framework_agente: K3 Trans
benchmarks_utilizados: RustRepoTrans repository-level C-to-Rust code translation benchmark
metricas_desempenho: Compilation@1, Pass@1, DSR@1, Repairable Ratio, CodeBLEU, AST Match Score
baseline_comparado: Basic RAG adapted from prior work, prompt engineering baseline, C2Rust rule-based baseline, ablations over knowledge types and retrieval strategy.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Knowledge base is evaluated at 0%, 50%, and 100% self-evolution stages; 100% corresponds to full K3 Trans.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; implementation limits LLM repair to one iteration for cost-effectiveness but reports no monetary cost.
resultado_principal: K3 Trans improves repository-context code translation with triple knowledge retrieval and self-debugging. With Claude-3-5-Sonnet, K3 Trans reaches 75.7% Compilation@1, 67.7% Pass@1, 82.1% DSR@1, 44.6% RR, 0.733 CodeBLEU, and 0.562 AST match.
desempenho_vs_baseline: For Claude-3-5-Sonnet, K3 Trans improves over prompt engineering from 57.9% to 75.7% Compilation@1 and from 49.1% to 67.7% Pass@1; the paper reports relative improvements up to 135.9% on Pass@1 and 32.8% on CodeBLEU among studied LLMs.
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Too many target-language code samples can introduce noise; benchmark target language is Rust only; possible data leakage between RustRepoTrans and target-language sample sources; one-iteration repair is used for cost-effectiveness.
ameacas_validade: Rust-only benchmark, possible overlap between benchmark and retrieved code samples, retrieval strategy settings, translation order randomness, and LLM choice may affect generalizability.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes ExpeRepair accumulating historical repair experiences into episodic and semantic memories, retrieving them at inference time for repository-level program repair, and evaluating on SWE-bench Lite.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 005; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Fangwen Mu, Junjie Wang, Lin Shi, Song Wang, Shoubin Li, Qing Wang
titulo: EXPEREPAIR: Dual-Memory Enhanced LLM-based Repository-Level Program Repair
formato_armazenamento: Dual memory with episodic memory storing concrete repair demonstrations and semantic memory storing abstract reflective repair insights; memories are updated after repair trajectories and retrieved during test generation and patch generation.
framework_agente: ExpeRepair
llm_utilizado: Claude 3.5 Sonnet V2 and Claude 3.7 Sonnet
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Lite
metricas_desempenho: Pass@1 / resolved rate; average inference cost; ESR; RSR; unique resolved issues; ablation resolved rates
baseline_comparado: SWE-Agent, Moatless Tools, Agentless-1.5, AutoCodeRover-v2, OpenHands, PatchPilot, DARS, and ablations without experience module, demonstrations, or insights.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Memory accumulates repair trajectories across processed issues; episodic retrieval uses top-5 semantically similar demonstrations.
custo_por_tarefa: Average inference cost is $2.07 per SWE-bench Lite instance with Claude 3.5 Sonnet V2.
resultado_principal: ExpeRepair achieves 47.7% pass@1 on SWE-bench Lite with Claude 3.5 Sonnet V2 at $2.07 average cost, and the abstract reports 49.3% pass@1 with Claude 3.7 Sonnet.
desempenho_vs_baseline: ExpeRepair surpasses PatchPilot 45.3% and DARS 47.0% on SWE-bench Lite while costing far less than DARS ($2.07 vs $12.24). Removing the experience module drops resolved rate from 47.7% to 41.3%.
custo_vs_baseline: DARS has comparable 47.0% resolved rate but nearly six times higher average cost ($12.24 vs $2.07).
limitacoes: Less emphasis on bug localization; memories may contain a narrow subset of successful localization cases, limiting diversity and coverage.
ameacas_validade: SWE-bench Lite as sole benchmark, historical repair trajectory selection, LLM choice, cost estimates, and comparison to published baseline numbers may affect validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes CTIM-Rover as a software-engineering agent with cross-task-instance episodic memory distilled from prior SWE-bench experiences and reports negative empirical results, which remain eligible evidence.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `lindenbauer2025ctimrover` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_experience_bank. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Tobias Lindenbauer, Georg Groh, Hinrich Schütze
titulo: From Knowledge to Noise: CTIM-Rover and the Pitfalls of Episodic Memory in Software Engineering Agents
formato_armazenamento: CTIM items (regras textuais destiladas de trajetórias bem-sucedidas via Knowledge Distillation com o1; general + repository-level) + trajetórias exemplares (full trajectory text) armazenadas em índice Milvus (Code-T5 embeddings)
framework_agente: AutoCodeRover (estendido com Reflexion)
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified (subconjunto: 401 treino, 45 teste)
metricas_desempenho: Success rate (% instâncias resolvidas)
baseline_comparado: AutoCodeRover (sem CTIM), configurações ablativas (CTIM only, General CTIM only, Repo-level CTIM only, Exemplar only)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Limitado por constraint sqrt(n); CTIM cresce sublinearmente com número de trajetórias disponíveis
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: AutoCodeRover 42% vs CTIM-Rover 40% (melhor config); CTIM only degrada para 31%. Memória episódica introduz ruído que degrada performance; itens CTIM ruidosos desviam o agente de explorações produtivas.
desempenho_vs_baseline: -2pp vs baseline (40% vs 42%); CTIM only -11pp (31% vs 42%)
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Amostra de teste pequena (45 instâncias); ~10% amostras removidas por problemas de ambiente; KD limitado a pares de trajetórias; CTIM não filtrado por relevância ao problema; não investigou retrieval baseado em embeddings como solução para ruído
ameacas_validade: Subset de SWE-bench pode não ser representativo; AutoCodeRover como único framework; distribuição desbalanceada de repositórios (django domina treino e teste); GPT-4o pode não ser ideal para destilação de conhecimento

full_text_rationale: Full text describes Git Context Controller as a persistent, version-controlled memory workspace for LLM agents, supporting cross-session continuity, branching, merging, and SWE-Bench/BrowseComp evaluation.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `wu2025gcc` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_persistent_workspace_state. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Junde Wu, Minhao Hu, Jiayuan Zhu, Jiazhen Pan, Yuyuan Liu, Min Xu, Yueming Jin
titulo: Git Context Controller: Manage the Context of LLM-based Agents like Git
formato_armazenamento: .GCC/ directory versionado: main.md (roadmap global, milestones, to-do list compartilhado entre branches), branches/{name}/commit.md (sumários de progresso: Branch Purpose + Previous Progress + This Commit's Contribution), log.md (traces OTA fine-grained), metadata.yaml (file structure, dependencies, configs). Tudo plain-text, checkpointed via git commits reais.
framework_agente: swe_agent (GCC integrado sobre SWE-Agent para SWE-Bench)
llm_utilizado: GPT-5, GPT-4.1, DeepSeek-V3.1, GLM-4.5-Air, Qwen3-235B-A22B, Claude 4 Sonnet
benchmarks_utilizados: BrowseComp-Plus (150 instâncias), SWE-Bench Verified (500 instâncias), SWE-Benchlite (300 instâncias)
metricas_desempenho: Pass@1, % Resolved, Tool Calls, % Correct Location (Line, Function, File)
baseline_comparado: ReAct Agent, SWE Agent, Summary Agent, Folding Agent (controlados); 26 sistemas existentes em SWE-Benchlite (CodeStory Aide, MarsCode, Honeycomb, MentatBot, etc.)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Context length não-monotônico: cresce com inference steps mas decresce periodicamente via summarization e retrieval; até 100 branches/archives sumarizados (max teórico 3.27M tokens)
custo_por_tarefa: $0.57/task (GPT-3.5), $1.13/task (GPT-4o), $1.21/task (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) em SWE-Benchlite
resultado_principal: SOTA em SWE-Bench Verified (Pass@1 0.802 com Claude 4 Sonnet, +13.6% vs baseline) e BrowseComp-Plus (0.834 com GPT-5). Em SWE-Benchlite, resolve 48.00% (144/300), superando 26 sistemas existentes incluindo agentes comerciais.
desempenho_vs_baseline: +6.2pp Pass@1 vs Folding Agent (melhor baseline) com Claude 4 Sonnet em SWE-Bench Verified (0.802 vs 0.740); +1.9pp BrowseComp-Plus com GPT-5 (0.834 vs 0.815)
custo_vs_baseline: $1.21/task vs $1.62 SWE-Agent, $2.51 Agentless em SWE-Benchlite (Claude 3.5 S); custo comparável aos melhores open-source apesar de performance muito superior
limitacoes: Context window fixo de 327K; retrieval window K=1 limita acesso a commits antigos; BRANCH e MERGE aumentam tool calls significativamente; não testado com budget fixo de custo
ameacas_validade: Modelos proprietários de última geração (GPT-5, Claude 4 Sonnet) podem ter capacidades emergentes não generalizáveis; benchmarks artificiais vs produção real; dependência de context windows grandes (327K); ablation apenas com Claude 4 Sonnet

full_text_rationale: Full text describes the Huxley-Godel Machine maintaining a tree/archive of self-modified coding agents and evaluations, using clade-level metaproductivity to guide future self-improvement, and evaluating on SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Lite, and Polyglot.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 005; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Wenyi Wang, Piotr Piekos, Nanbo Li, Firas Laakom, Yimeng Chen, Mateusz Ostaszewski, Mingchen Zhuge, Jurgen Schmidhuber
titulo: Huxley-Gödel Machine: Human-Level Coding Agent Development by an Approximation of the Optimal Self-Improving Machine
formato_armazenamento: Tree/archive of self-modified coding agents with clade-level metaproductivity estimates; descendant performance aggregates guide which agents to expand or evaluate.
framework_agente: Huxley-Godel Machine (HGM)
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified-60, SWE-bench Lite, Polyglot
metricas_desempenho: Accuracy; allocated CPU-hours for 800 evaluations; Pearson correlation of CMP estimates; leaderboard/transfer accuracy
baseline_comparado: SICA, DGM, initial ancestor, SWE-agent + GPT-5-mini, SWE-agent leaderboard baseline, HGM transfer variants.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Archive is a self-modification tree grown under an 800-evaluation budget in the main comparison.
custo_por_tarefa: NR as monetary cost; reports allocated CPU-hours: HGM 517 hours on SWE-Verified-60 and 347 hours on Polyglot for 800 evaluations.
resultado_principal: HGM outperforms prior self-improving coding-agent development methods while using fewer allocated CPU-hours. Table 2 reports HGM 56.7% on SWE-Verified-60 and 30.5% on Polyglot, compared with DGM 53.3% and 27.1%.
desempenho_vs_baseline: HGM requires 517 CPU-hours versus DGM 1231 on SWE-Verified-60, and 347 versus DGM 2385 on Polyglot; on SWE-Lite standard, the HGM best-belief agent reaches 49.0% with GPT-5-mini and 57.0% with GPT-5 transfer.
custo_vs_baseline: HGM is reported as 2.38x faster than DGM on SWE-Verified-60 and 6.86x faster than DGM on Polyglot in allocated CPU-hours.
limitacoes: Theoretical result depends on simplifying assumptions; evaluations are costly and partially observed; optimized agents may overfit benchmark-specific behavior; results depend on selected backbones and budgets.
ameacas_validade: SWE-Verified-60 subset, Polyglot setup, local leaderboard comparisons, backbone substitutions, and CPU-hour accounting may affect external validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes repository memory from historical commits, issues, and functionality summaries as episodic/semantic memory for code-localization agents, evaluated on SWE-bench Verified and SWE-bench Live.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `wang2025repositorymemory` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_repository_memory. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Boshi Wang, Weijian Xu, Yunsheng Li, Mei Gao, Yujia Xie, Huan Sun, Dongdong Chen
titulo: Improving Code Localization with Repository Memory
formato_armazenamento: Corpus de commits recentes (episódica: mensagens + patches + issues linkadas, buscável via BM25 com tokenizer LLM) + sumários de funcionalidade de arquivos ativos (semântica: texto NL gerado por LLM para top-200 arquivos mais editados)
framework_agente: LocAgent (framework de localização baseado em grafo heterogêneo)
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench-verified (500 exemplos, 12 repositórios), SWE-bench-live (130 exemplos, 62 repositórios)
metricas_desempenho: Accuracy@1, Accuracy@3, Accuracy@5
baseline_comparado: CodeRankEmbed, Agentless, LocAgent (sem memória)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: 7000 commits + 200 arquivos mais editados por repositório; tamanho fixo por design (janela fixa de commits recentes)
custo_por_tarefa: $0.58-$0.89/exemplo (RepoMem) vs $0.54-$0.59/exemplo (LocAgent); custo maior concentrado em problemas difíceis onde LocAgent falha
resultado_principal: Acc@5 76.5% no SWE-bench-verified (+4.9% vs LocAgent); memória episódica e semântica contribuem de forma complementar; agente muda comportamento de exploração bruta para investigação guiada por hipóteses
desempenho_vs_baseline: +4.9% Acc@5 absoluto vs LocAgent em SWE-bench-verified; +3.1% em SWE-bench-live; resolve rate +3.4% em end-to-end (40.4% vs 37.0%)
custo_vs_baseline: Custo maior em problemas difíceis ($0.54→$0.89 quando LocAgent falha); alta variância por exemplo; memória é investimento estratégico em problemas difíceis
limitacoes: Performance degrada em repositórios com histórico limitado (<10K commits); custo variável e dependente do problema; método de recuperação não otimizado para vocabulário de código; links explícitos entre commits e issues podem ser ruidosos ou ausentes
ameacas_validade: Avaliação apenas em bug fixing/localização; GPT-4o como único modelo; commits pré-issue podem ter qualidade variável; janela fixa de 7K commits pode não ser ideal para todos os repositórios

full_text_rationale: Full text describes an agentic kernel-coding framework with a persistent knowledge base and metadata store that enable continuous knowledge accumulation, checkpointing, and cross-session knowledge reuse, and it evaluates correctness/performance on KernelBench and production operator-platform configurations.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 005; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: KernelEvolve Team, Meta Platforms
titulo: KernelEvolve: Scaling Agentic Kernel Coding for Heterogeneous AI Accelerators at Meta
formato_armazenamento: Persistent knowledge base of hardware-specific constraints and optimization patterns plus metadata/object stores for kernel candidates, parent-child relationships, fitness scores, correctness flags, artifacts, profiling reports, and checkpointed search state.
framework_agente: KernelEvolve production kernel-coding framework
llm_utilizado: Claude 4.5, GPT-5, Meta CWM, Llama, and internal/external LLM backends
benchmarks_utilizados: KernelBench, 160 PyTorch ATen operators across NVIDIA GPUs, AMD GPUs, and MTIA v3, Meta production recommendation-model workloads
metricas_desempenho: Pass rate/correctness; speedup over PyTorch baselines; development time; operator-platform correctness; metadata query scalability; production deployment outcomes
baseline_comparado: PyTorch baselines, expert manual implementations, existing kernel-generation prototypes such as KernelBench/KernelLLM/CWM-related systems.
tokens_entrada_por_tarefa: NR; context windows are managed within 64K-1M token budgets depending on the model.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Search graph and persistent stores scale to thousands of optimization steps; metadata queries execute in milliseconds even with large search histories.
custo_por_tarefa: NR as per-task monetary cost; reports development time reduced from weeks to hours and production speedups up to 17x.
reducao_tokens: NR; paper states persistent storage reduces redundant token consumption and future work will quantify token consumption per kernel.
resultado_principal: KernelEvolve achieves 100% pass rate on all 250 KernelBench problems and 100% correctness over 480 operator-platform configurations, while reducing development time from weeks to hours and achieving up to 17x speedup over PyTorch baselines.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Reports 1.2x to 17x speedups over PyTorch baselines across production use cases and competitive performance with expert manual implementations.
custo_vs_baseline: Development time is reduced from weeks to hours; no per-task API cost is reported.
limitacoes: Production deployment depends on proprietary hardware, persistent internal knowledge bases, and validation infrastructure; future work is needed to quantify token consumption and optimize prompt design for minimal inference cost.
ameacas_validade: Meta-specific workloads, hardware platforms, proprietary accelerators, production validation stack, and unpublished source code may limit independent replication.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes Lingxi extracting transferable procedural knowledge from historical issue-fixing data and using it to guide repository-level issue resolution, with SWE-bench Verified evaluation.

autores: Xu Yang, Jiayuan Zhou, Michael Pacheco, Wenhan Zhu, Pengfei He, Shaowei Wang, Kui Liu, Ruiqi Pan
titulo: Lingxi: Repository-Level Issue Resolution Framework Enhanced by Procedural Knowledge Guided Scaling
formato_armazenamento: Transferable procedural knowledge extracted from historical issue-fixing data; target issue summaries are embedded, similar historical issues are retrieved and reranked, and procedural knowledge guides analysis scaling and patch generation.
framework_agente: Lingxi repository-level issue-resolution framework
llm_utilizado: Claude 4 Sonnet, Kimi-K2, GPT-4.1 mini, Qwen3-Embedding-8B, Qwen3-Reranker-4B
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified
metricas_desempenho: Pass@1 resolve rate; ablation pass rate; model/backbone comparison
baseline_comparado: mini-SWE-agent, OpenHands, SWE-agent, SWE-search, Augment Agent prior, raw issue-patch memory and no-knowledge ablations
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Historical issue-fixing data are converted into reusable procedural knowledge; no explicit memory-growth curve is reported.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: Lingxi reaches 74.60% Pass@1 on SWE-bench Verified with Claude 4 Sonnet and 70.33% with Kimi-K2. Ablations show the full method at 74.80%, while replacing transferable procedural knowledge with raw issue-patch pairs drops to 68.30% and removing knowledge drops to 67.74%.
desempenho_vs_baseline: With Claude 4 Sonnet, Lingxi reports 74.60% versus OpenHands and Augment Agent prior at 70.40%, SWE-search at 70.08%, SWE-agent at 66.60%, and mini-SWE-agent at 64.93%. With Kimi-K2, Lingxi reports 70.33% versus OpenHands 65.40% and mini-SWE-agent 43.80%.
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Evaluation centers on SWE-bench Verified; no cost/token reporting captured; method depends on quality and coverage of historical issue-fixing data and LLM-derived procedural knowledge.
ameacas_validade: SWE-bench Verified focus, retrieval/reranking choices, historical issue-pair construction, and proprietary/closed model backbones may affect generalization and replication.

contextual_role: Security/adverse-effect evidence about persistent poisoned experience retrieval in an agent memory store.
full_text_rationale: Full text studies MemoryGraft attacks on long-term experience retrieval, showing poisoned memories persist alongside benign experiences and cause behavioral drift across later sessions in a DataInterpreter/code setting.
extractor_notes: Rechecked prior extraction against search inclusion role; security/adverse-effect contextual record.
autores: Saksham Sahai Srivastava, Haoyu He
titulo: MemoryGraft: Persistent Compromise of LLM Agents via Poisoned Experience Retrieval
formato_armazenamento: NA — paper de segurança que analisa vulnerabilidades em memory stores de agentes LLM. Ataca MetaGPT DataInterpreter com RAG store envenenado (BM25 + FAISS vector index) contendo 100 experiências benignas + 10 envenenadas.
framework_agente: NA — avalia ataque contra MetaGPT DataInterpreter
benchmarks_utilizados: Custom evaluation set (12 consultas handcrafted para MetaGPT DataInterpreter, 110 experience seeds)
metricas_desempenho: Poisoned Retrieval Proportion (PRP), behavioral drift qualitativo
baseline_comparado: MetaGPT DataInterpreter sem memória envenenada, BM25-only vs BM25+FAISS union retrieval
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: NA
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: MemoryGraft demonstra que 10 experiências envenenadas em 110 totais (9.1%) alcançam Poisoned Retrieval Proportion de 47.9%, comprometendo persistentemente o comportamento do agente. Ataque persiste entre sessões, é trigger-free e explora semantic imitation heuristic.
desempenho_vs_baseline: PRP 47.9% com union retrieval (BM25+FAISS) vs ~25% com BM25-only; 23 de 48 retrievals totais retornam experiências envenenadas
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Avaliação em escala pequena (110 seeds, 12 queries); apenas MetaGPT DataInterpreter como alvo; apenas GPT-4o; design do ataque é semi-white-box (atacante sabe quais artefatos o agente ingere); sem métricas de severidade comportamental quantitativas; defesas propostas não avaliadas empiricamente
ameacas_validade: Apenas um framework de agente (MetaGPT); GPT-4o como único LLM; 12 queries de avaliação handcrafted; cenário de ataque assume que agente executa código de README sem verificação; escala pequena pode não representar deployments reais

contextual_role: Benchmark evidence for long-term memory and state tracking in multi-platform agent environments.

autores: Darshan Deshpande, Varun Gangal, Hersh Mehta, Anand Kannappan, Rebecca Qian, Peng Wang
titulo: MemTrack: Evaluating Long-Term Memory and State Tracking in Multi-Platform Dynamic Agent Environments
venue: NeurIPS 2025 Workshop: Scaling Environments for Agents (SEA)
formato_armazenamento: NA — benchmark agnóstico ao backend; avalia Mem0 (ChromaDB, embeddings) e Zep (grafo de conhecimento temporal) como componentes de memória
framework_agente: NA — avalia agentes com GPT-5 e Gemini-2.5-Pro + ferramentas multi-plataforma (Slack, Linear, Git)
llm_utilizado: GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-4o-mini (embeddings para Mem0), GPT-4o (LLM-judge)
benchmarks_utilizados: MemTrack (47 instâncias curadas, multi-plataforma: Slack, Linear, Git em ambiente dockerizado)
metricas_desempenho: Correctness (LLM-judge), Efficiency (tool call count), Redundancy (tool call overlap), tool call entropy
baseline_comparado: LLM+NoMem, LLM+Mem0, LLM+Zep (para GPT-5 e Gemini-2.5-Pro)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: NA
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: LLMs de fronteira como GPT-5 atingem apenas ~60% de Correctness no MemTrack; componentes de memória (Mem0, Zep) não melhoram desempenho significativamente e aumentam redundância de tool calls (~21%). Desempenho degrada em perguntas de follow-up.
desempenho_vs_baseline: GPT-5+Mem0 0.610 vs GPT-5+NoMem 0.601 (+1.5%); Gemini+Mem0 0.118 vs Gemini+NoMem 0.144 (-18%); Zep sem ganho significativo
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Dataset limitado a 47 instâncias; apenas dois LLMs avaliados; Mem0 e Zep usados com configurações padrão sem otimização; ambiente simulado pode não capturar complexidade total de workflows reais; sem avaliação de latência ou custo
ameacas_validade: Apenas GPT-5 e Gemini-2.5-Pro testados; Mem0 e Zep com configurações padrão; LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) para Correctness pode introduzir viés; cenários simulados vs workflows empresariais reais; 5 runs para significância estatística em dataset pequeno
