limitacoes: Analysis assumes self-experience in experiments, focuses on selected math/web/SWE settings, and leaves more sophisticated closed-loop memory collection and integration for future work.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on base model, embedding model, memory construction from sampled trajectories, K and consolidation hyperparameters, benchmark mix, and plotted rather than fully tabulated task-level values.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes an agentic Docker-building pipeline with a global/cross-task success memory pool of verified environment solutions reused across repository-level environment-construction tasks, with Multi-Docker-Eval and downstream SWE/Terminal benchmark evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 009; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Jiaran Zhang, Luck Ma, Yanhao Li, Fanqi Wan, Di Qi, Xu Zhao, Jieyi Hou, Zhe Xie, Mengqiang Ren, Xin Wu, Zhewei Huang, Liangyu Chen, Yingwei Ma, Qi Han, Xiangyu Zhang
titulo: DockSmith: Scaling Reliable Coding Environments via an Agentic Docker Builder
formato_armazenamento: SWE-Factory global memory pool of validated Dockerfile and evaluation-script pairs, augmented for cross-task retrieval so verified environment solutions can serve as demonstrations for new repositories.
framework_agente: SWE-Factory-style multi-agent Docker-building pipeline with Context Retrieval, Dockerfile Generation, Eval Scripts Handling, Test Analysis, loop detector, and cross-task success memory
llm_utilizado: Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct; GPT-5.1 used as automated annotator for error analysis
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; ablation; error analysis; case study
benchmarks_utilizados: Multi-Docker-Eval, SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Multilingual, Terminal-Bench 2.0
metricas_desempenho: Fail-to-Pass, Commit Rate, average input tokens, average output tokens, average Docker image count, SWE.V/SWE.M/Terminal scores, error counts, critical errors, terminal rate, resolution rate
baseline_comparado: DeepSeek-v3.1, DeepSeek-R1, GPT-OSS-20B, GPT-OSS-120B, Kimi-K2 variants, Qwen3-235B-A22B, Claude-Sonnet-4, GPT-5-Mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct, SWE-only and Docker-only training ablations, acceptance-shaping and curriculum ablations
tokens_entrada_por_tarefa: On Multi-Docker-Eval, DockSmith reports average input 207.68 versus Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct 150.10, DeepSeek-v3.1 158.11, and GPT-5-Mini 339.94.
tokens_saida_por_tarefa: On Multi-Docker-Eval, DockSmith reports average output 26.38 versus Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct 13.75, DeepSeek-v3.1 17.15, and GPT-5-Mini 103.32.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Memory pool contains verified environment solutions generated from accepted, test-backed pull requests and is reused cross-task during trajectory generation; exact final pool size is NR.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; reports token/process metrics and controlled training-token mixing ratios but no monetary cost.
reducao_tokens: NR; DockSmith uses more input/output tokens than the base Qwen3-Coder baseline on Multi-Docker-Eval but achieves higher success.
resultado_principal: DockSmith reaches 39.72% Fail-to-Pass and 58.28% Commit Rate on Multi-Docker-Eval, exceeding evaluated open- and closed-source baselines and the prior reported upper bound of 37.7% F2P.
desempenho_vs_baseline: DockSmith improves +20.3 absolute F2P points over Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct. Error analysis reduces total errors from 3,757 to 2,161, critical errors by 40.2%, and average errors per trajectory from 11.81 to 6.67.
custo_vs_baseline: Compared with Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct, DockSmith increases average input tokens from 150.10 to 207.68 and output tokens from 13.75 to 26.38 while improving F2P from 19.46% to 39.72%.
limitacoes: Training and evaluation emphasize environment construction; some languages remain bottlenecked by compiler toolchains and system dependencies; context-retrieval errors increase under higher-recall retrieval.
ameacas_validade: Benchmark composition, automated GPT-5.1 error annotation, training-data filtering, token-level mixing ratio, and use of Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct as base model may affect validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes DomAgent for domain-specific code generation with knowledge-graph retrieval and case-based reasoning over stored code cases, evaluated on DS-1000 and real-world truck software-development code-generation tasks with ablations.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 009; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Shuai Wang, Dhasarathy Parthasarathy, Robert Feldt, Yinan Yu
titulo: DomAgent: Leveraging Knowledge Graphs and Case-Based Reasoning for Domain-Specific Code Generation
formato_armazenamento: Domain knowledge is stored as a knowledge graph of packages, functions, descriptions, parameters, and relations; the case base stores executable code cases in a vector database keyed by natural-language task descriptions after KG-guided coverage selection.
framework_agente: DomAgent with DomRetriever, SearchKG and SearchCase tool calls, KG-guided case selection, case reranking, LLM-guided filtering, and GRPO-trained retrieval use
benchmarks_utilizados: DS-1000 and Truck CAN Signal code generation dataset with 776 CAN-signal tasks across six domains
metricas_desempenho: pass@1 by library/domain, total pass@1, sampling proportion versus pass@1
baseline_comparado: Vanilla Qwen2.5-7B, LLaMA3.1-8B, LLaMA3.3-70B, GPT-4o, KG-only, CBR-only, KG+CBR RAG pipeline, StarCoder, WizardCoder, CodeLLaMA-Python, WizardCoder-CL, Magicoder-CL, MagicoderS-CL, random case sampling
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: DS-1000 case-base construction samples 300 examples (30%) for case bases and evaluates on 700 examples; Truck CAN Signal uses 776 tasks over six domains. The case base is a small representative subset selected for package and functional-cluster coverage.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: DomAgent improves DS-1000 total pass@1 to 39.2% with Qwen2.5-7B and 40.5% with LLaMA3.1-8B, and DomRetriever plus GPT-4o reaches 58.6%. On Truck CAN Signal, Qwen2.5-7B DomAgent reaches 96.64% total pass@1 and DomRetriever plus GPT-4o reaches 98.04%.
desempenho_vs_baseline: On Truck CAN Signal, Qwen2.5-7B improves from 39.62% to 96.64% total pass@1, and GPT-4o with DomRetriever improves from 71.22% to 98.04%. On DS-1000, Qwen2.5-7B improves from 29.3% to 39.2% total pass@1; external GPT-4o improves from 51.0% to 58.6% with LLaMA3.1-8B DomRetriever.
custo_vs_baseline: NR; case selection reduces manual case-base burden by achieving comparable performance with 30% KG-selected cases versus 80% random sampling.
limitacoes: Future work is needed for AST-based fine-grained similarity, soft prompts, and broader domain adaptation; the truck dataset uses internal documentation and domain-specific infrastructure.
ameacas_validade: Internal truck dataset access, manually curated representative cases and prompts, choice of KG structure, reward model from external LLM, RL training setup, and comparison across different code-generation backbones may affect validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes EET distilling historical issue-resolution executions into an experience base retrieved for later software-engineering agent decisions, with SWE-bench Verified evaluation.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `guo2026eet` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_experience_bank. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Yaoqi Guo, Ying Xiao, Jie M. Zhang, Mark Harman, Yiling Lou, Yang Liu, Zhenpeng Chen
titulo: EET: Experience-Driven Early Termination for Cost-Efficient Software Engineering Agents
formato_armazenamento: Experience objects estruturados (JSON: issue_id, task_description, execution_summary, evaluation_result, confidence, confidence_reason); abstrações de trajetórias de resolução bem-sucedidas armazenadas em experience base
framework_agente: custom (EET — plugin agnóstico avaliado sobre Agentless, Mini-SWE-Agent, Trae Agent)
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified (500 tarefas; 207 de SWE-bench Lite para geração de experiência)
metricas_desempenho: % Resolved, # API Calls, # Input Tokens, # Output Tokens, Total Cost ($)
baseline_comparado: Agentes sem EET (Agentless, Mini-SWE-Agent, Trae Agent), Turn-control (Gao and Peng 2026), Naive-EET (RAG com trajetórias brutas)
tokens_entrada_por_tarefa: Varia por agente: Agentless ~72K/task, Mini-SWE ~176K/task, Trae ~3.6M/task (sem EET); redução média de 29.9% com EET
tokens_saida_por_tarefa: Varia por agente: Agentless ~3.7K/task, Mini-SWE ~7.6K/task, Trae ~46.4K/task (sem EET); redução média de 25.1% com EET
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Experiências geradas de 207 tarefas SWE-bench Lite; base fixa para avaliação (não cresce durante teste)
custo_por_tarefa: Agentless GPT-5-mini: $0.028→$0.012/task (-55.1%); Trae GPT-5-mini: $0.324→$0.232/task (-28.2%); média -31.8% em 6 configurações
reducao_tokens: -29.9% input tokens, -25.1% output tokens, -20.8% API calls em média
resultado_principal: EET reduz custo total em 19.3-55.1% (média 31.8%) em 3 agentes × 2 LLMs, com perda negligível de resolve rate (máximo 0.2%). Early termination identifica oportunidades em 11.3% dos issues em média.
desempenho_vs_baseline: +2.7% resolve rate médio vs agentes sem EET; Agentless beneficia mais (+7.5% abs com GPT-5-mini)
custo_vs_baseline: -31.8% custo total médio vs sem EET; vs Turn-control: -31.8% vs -41.4% (Turn-control reduz mais custo mas degrada resolve rate em -10.7%)
limitacoes: Avaliação limitada a SWE-bench Verified; dependência de dados históricos para cold start em domínios novos; transferibilidade cross-agent não testada; experiências são específicas para cada agente
ameacas_validade: SWE-bench Verified como único benchmark; GPT-5-mini e DeepSeek-V3.2 como únicos modelos; experiências geradas de SWE-bench Lite podem não representar issues reais em produção; threshold tuning no mesmo dataset pode superestimar ganhos

full_text_rationale: Full text describes EffiSkill mining reusable optimization skills from slow/fast program pairs, storing them in a portable skill library, and retrieving/applying them in LLM-based code-efficiency optimization agents on unseen programs.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 009; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Zimu Wang, Yuling Shi, Mengfan Li, Zijun Liu, Jie M. Zhang, Chengcheng Wan, Xiaodong Gu
titulo: EffiSkill: Agent Skill Based Automated Code Efficiency Optimization
formato_armazenamento: Portable skill library mined offline from 900 Python and 900 C++ slow/fast program pairs; stores Operator Skill Cards with transformation mechanisms and Meta Skill Cards with procedural guidance for selecting and composing mechanisms.
framework_agente: EffiSkill two-stage framework with offline skill mining and execution-free skill-guided optimization
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; ablation; task-level comparison; skill-library analysis; case study
benchmarks_utilizados: EffiBench-X Python and C++ subsets from 623 optimization tasks
metricas_desempenho: OPT@1, OPT@8, paired bootstrap significance, task win/tie/loss counts, no-improvement distribution, runtime comparison, skill-family usage distribution
metricas_cl: Skill usage distribution, operator-skill family summary, recurring skill bundles, candidate-skill pairs, problem-bundle observations
baseline_comparado: Instruction, RAG, CoT, SBLLM, FasterPy, EffiCoder, ablations without retrieval, with random skills, and without multi-plan composition
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: The mined library contains 29 operator skills; the top five account for about 68.0-70.0% of usage. Mining corpus has 900 Python and 900 C++ slow/fast pairs with speedup ratio at least 2.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; all methods use a fixed candidate budget of k=8 candidates per task and execution-free inference.
resultado_principal: EffiSkill achieves the best OPT@1 and OPT@8 across GPT-5-mini and Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct in Python and C++. It improves over the strongest non-EffiSkill baseline by 12.03 points on C++ and 4.98 on Python with GPT-5-mini, and by 12.36 and 8.67 points with Qwen3-Coder.
desempenho_vs_baseline: With GPT-5-mini, EffiSkill reaches Python OPT@1/OPT@8 of 26.48/37.40 and C++ OPT@1/OPT@8 of 44.62/66.77. With Qwen3-Coder, it reaches Python 21.35/36.60 and C++ 34.19/56.50, outperforming Instruction, RAG, CoT, SBLLM, FasterPy, and EffiCoder where applicable.
custo_vs_baseline: All methods use the same k=8 candidate budget; EffiSkill is execution-free during generation and does not rely on online runtime feedback inside the optimization loop.
limitacoes: Evaluation is on competitive-programming efficiency tasks; real-world software systems may involve libraries, compiler behavior, hardware variation, and cross-file context; broader validation across models, languages, and domains is still needed.
ameacas_validade: Skill quality depends on LLM-generated traces, clustering choices, filtering thresholds, candidate budget, ranking setup, execution environment, and possible distributional similarity between mining and evaluation corpora despite direct-overlap checks.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes FailureMem storing historical repair trajectories in a hierarchical failure memory bank and retrieving them as reusable guidance for multimodal automated program repair on SWE-bench Multimodal.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 010; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Ruize Ma, Yilei Jiang, Shilin Zhang, Zheng Ma, Yi Feng, Vincent Ng, Zhi Wang, Xiangyu Yue, Chuanyi Li, Lewei Lu
titulo: FailureMem: A Failure-Aware Multimodal Framework for Autonomous Software Repair
formato_armazenamento: Failure Memory Bank built from failed multimodal repair trajectories. Each memory entry has contextual, cognitive, and code layers covering issue summary, visual analysis, diagnosis, negative constraint, golden principle, failed patch summary, and golden patch summary.
framework_agente: FailureMem hybrid workflow-agent architecture with active perception tools and a Failure Memory Bank for multimodal automated program repair
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; ablation; component analysis; retrieval-depth sensitivity
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Multimodal with 617 GitHub issues across 17 JavaScript repositories; development split used to build the memory bank
metricas_desempenho: resolved rate, pass@1 averaged over three runs, solved instance counts, top-k memory sensitivity, component ablation
metricas_cl: Memory-bank construction from 84 failed development trajectories; contextual/cognitive/code layer ablations; retrieval top-k sensitivity from k=1 to k=10
baseline_comparado: GUIRepair, Agentless Lite, SWE-agent, Zencoder, Computer-Use Agents, commercial systems, and FailureMem ablations
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: 84 structured memory entries built from failed trajectories after excluding 18 successful memory-free repairs from 102 SWE-bench Multimodal development issues; default retrieval k=3.
custo_por_tarefa: USD 0.33 per issue for FailureMem versus USD 0.29 for GUIRepair, reported as about 13% higher inference cost.
resultado_principal: FailureMem improves resolved rate over GUIRepair on SWE-bench Multimodal, with the abstract reporting a 3.7 point gain. The full GPT-5.1 configuration reaches 33.1% versus 29.4% for GUIRepair.
desempenho_vs_baseline: FailureMem reaches 31.1%, 33.1%, 33.3%, 32.5%, and 33.8% resolved rates with GPT-4.1, GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2, Claude 4, and Claude 4.5 respectively, compared with GUIRepair at 28.8%, 29.4%, 29.8%, 28.6%, and 31.5%.
custo_vs_baseline: FailureMem reports higher inference cost than GUIRepair, USD 0.33 versus USD 0.29 per issue, while adding active perception and memory guidance.
limitacoes: Detailed memory contexts increase inference cost; performance depends on diversity and coverage of the offline failure-memory bank; rare or unprecedented failures may lack similar retrieved memories; automated repair still requires regression checks and human oversight.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on SWE-bench Multimodal, model choice, memory-bank construction from development failures, retrieval depth, visual-grounding tools, and GPU resources; larger or different GUI repositories may change memory usefulness.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes Group-Evolving Agents maintaining an archive and sharing evolutionary traces, patches, logs, outcomes, and learned artifacts across parent and offspring coding agents, with SWE-bench Verified and Polyglot evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 010; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Zhaotian Weng, Antonis Antoniades, Deepak Nathani, Zhen Zhang, Xiao Pu, Xin Eric Wang
titulo: Group-Evolving Agents: Open-Ended Self-Improvement via Experience Sharing
formato_armazenamento: Archive of evolving agent groups plus shared group-level experience, including framework code patches, predicted task patches, tool-use logs, workflows, execution outcomes, evaluation results, and learned artifacts.
framework_agente: Group-Evolving Agents (GEA) open-ended self-improvement framework with group-level parent selection and experience sharing
llm_utilizado: Claude Haiku 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4.5; OpenHands+GPT-5 and Aider+GPT-5 used for human-designed framework comparisons
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; self-evolution comparison; transfer analysis; robustness analysis; cost analysis
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Verified-Mini, Polyglot, 10-task sanity checks
metricas_desempenho: pass@1 success, final benchmark score, iterations to fix framework-level bugs, number of evolved agents, ancestor diversity, code changes, cost estimates
metricas_cl: Archive and ancestor diversity; top-agent lineage depth; experience sharing across parent group members; robustness to framework-level bugs
baseline_comparado: DGM-like self-evolving baseline, Darwin Godel Machine lineage, OpenHands+GPT-5, Aider+GPT-5, human-designed framework comparisons
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: GEA runs 30 iterations on SWE-bench and 20 on Polyglot, using groups of size K=2 and KNN M=4. The best GEA agent integrates 17 unique ancestors, about 28.3% of the population.
custo_por_tarefa: Full-run estimates are about USD 13,000 per method on SWE-bench and USD 1,500 on Polyglot; per-60-task evaluation examples include USD 370 for Claude Sonnet 4.5 on SWE-bench and USD 60 on Polyglot.
resultado_principal: GEA reaches 71.0% on SWE-bench Verified and 88.3% on Polyglot, outperforming the DGM-like baseline at 56.7% and 68.3% under the same evolved-agent budget.
desempenho_vs_baseline: GEA nearly matches OpenHands+GPT-5 on SWE-bench Verified (71.0% versus 71.8%) and exceeds Aider+GPT-5 on Polyglot (88.3% versus 52.0%). It fixes framework-level bugs in 1.4 iterations on average versus 5.0 for DGM trials.
custo_vs_baseline: GEA and the DGM-style baseline use the same number of evolved agents, so evaluation cost is reported as similar under the staged benchmark protocol.
limitacoes: Evaluation is expensive and relies on staged benchmark subsets; results depend on model choice, group size, parent-selection settings, and benchmark-specific evolution dynamics.
ameacas_validade: Staged selection from small sanity and mini-benchmark subsets may influence which agents reach full evaluation; benchmark scores depend on tool environments and large model APIs; human-designed framework comparisons are not identical training/evolution protocols.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes KAPSO, an autonomous program synthesis and optimization framework with a cognitive memory layer that stores reusable lessons from experiment traces and retrieves them across program-optimization tasks, evaluated on MLE-Bench and ALE-Bench.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `nadafian2026kapso` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_experience_bank. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Alireza Nadafian, Alireza Mohammadshahi, Majid Yazdani
titulo: KAPSO: A Knowledge-grounded framework for Autonomous Program Synthesis and Optimization
formato_armazenamento: Knowledge system: MediaWiki typed pages (Principle, Implementation, Environment, Heuristic) com Neo4j graph index + Weaviate vector index, ingerindo repos, docs, papers e web content de 2000+ fontes. Cognitive memory: episodic store em Weaviate vector DB com JSON fallback (trigger, lesson, recommended action, provenance para experiment branch). Experimentation engine: git branches isoladas com artifacts commitados.
framework_agente: custom (KAPSO: OrchestratorAgent + CodingAgent + SearchStrategy + ContextManager + KnowledgeSearch)
benchmarks_utilizados: MLE-Bench (Kaggle-style ML competitions), ALE-Bench (AtCoder heuristic contests, C++)
metricas_desempenho: Medal rate (%), Private score, Final Performance (ELO), Rank Percentile (%), Total Cost ($)
baseline_comparado: R&D-Agent, AIRA-dojo, ML-Master, AIDE (MLE-Bench); ALE-Agent, ALE Sequential, ALE one-shot (ALE-Bench)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Knowledge base fixa (pre-built); episodic memory cresce com experimentos executados; CTIM items limitados por capacidade configurável
custo_por_tarefa: ALE-Bench: $914.8 total (11 competições) ≈ $83/competição; MLE-Bench: budget $200/tarefa, 24h runtime
resultado_principal: MLE-Bench: 50.67% medal rate (vs 35.11% melhor open-source R&D-Agent, +15.56pp); ALE-Bench: ELO 1909.4, rank percentile 6.1% (vs ALE-Agent 1879.3, 6.8%) com custo 9% menor ($914.8 vs $1003.3).
desempenho_vs_baseline: +15.56pp medal rate vs R&D-Agent em MLE-Bench; +30.1 ELO vs ALE-Agent em ALE-Bench; vantagem concentrada em tarefas Hard (+17.78pp)
custo_vs_baseline: ALE-Bench: $914.8 vs $1003.3 ALE-Agent (-8.8%); MLE-Bench: orçamento fixo de $200 não permite comparação direta
limitacoes: Número limitado de competições no ALE-Bench introduz ruído; variância entre tarefas; dependência de LLMs proprietários (Gemini); custo elevado por tarefa; knowledge package pode ter viés de seleção
ameacas_validade: MLE-Bench e ALE-Bench são competições ML/algoritmos, não SE tradicional (bug fixing, code localization); Gemini-2.5-pro como único modelo; knowledge base construída pelos autores com critérios de seleção potencialmente subjetivos

full_text_rationale: Full text describes KernelBlaster using a persistent CUDA knowledge base that accumulates optimization experience for future CUDA kernel tasks, with a profile-guided evaluation pipeline.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `dong2026kernelblaster` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_structured_knowledge_base. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Kris Shengjun Dong, Sahil Modi, Dima Nikiforov, Sana Damani, Edward Lin, Siva Kumar Sastry Hari, Christos Kozyrakis
titulo: KernelBlaster: Continual Cross-Task CUDA Optimization via Memory-Augmented In-Context Reinforcement Learning
formato_armazenamento: Persistent CUDA Knowledge Base (~50KB JSON): hierarquia de estados de performance (memory-bound, compute-bound, hybrid) → listas de otimizações candidatas com scores de desempenho esperado. Cada entrada ⟨state, (optimization, score)⟩. Atualizada via PolicyEvaluation (compara resultado vs predição), PerfGapAnalysis (analisa discrepância), ParameterUpdate (reescreve KB).
framework_agente: custom
benchmarks_utilizados: KernelBench Level 1, Level 2, Level 3 (NVIDIA A6000, H100, L40S GPUs)
metricas_desempenho: Geometric mean speedup, fast_p (% kernels with ≥r× speedup), ValidRate
baseline_comparado: AI CUDA Engineer, IREE compiler, PyTorch (Eager + torch.compile), Kernelsseum (zero-shot), no_mem_agent (ablação)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: ~50KB Knowledge Base; cresce com estados descobertos (~5.5 estados médios por kernel); otimizações acumuladas ~60 únicas após 8000 tentativas
custo_por_tarefa: NR
reducao_tokens: 2.4× menos tokens que minimal agent sem Knowledge Base
resultado_principal: KernelBlaster atinge speedups geométricos médios de 1.43×, 2.50× e 1.50× nos KernelBench Level 1-3 vs PyTorch. Knowledge Base persistente permite transferência cross-task e cross-GPU, com 1.67× ganho sobre agente sem memória.
desempenho_vs_baseline: GeoMean 1.43× (L1), 2.50× (L2), 1.50× (L3) vs PyTorch; 1.67× vs no_mem_agent; supera AI CUDA Engineer em fast_p para speedups moderados a altos
custo_vs_baseline: 2.4× menos tokens que minimal agent sem KB; KB reutilizável entre GPUs (~50KB compacta)
limitacoes: Avaliação limitada a kernels CUDA NVIDIA; Level 3 tem scaling challenges com single optimization por iteração; Knowledge Base pode ter viés para entradas iniciais; dependência de NCU profiling detalhado para feedback de qualidade
ameacas_validade: KernelBench como único benchmark; GPUs NVIDIA específicas (Ampere, Hopper, Ada); reward hacking possível mesmo com verificação de corretude; comparação com AI CUDA Engineer sob condições de avaliação diferentes (modelos, hardware, test harness)

full_text_rationale: Full text describes KernelSkill's two-level memory architecture: a retrievable long-term kernel-optimization knowledge memory reused across kernels and a short-term trajectory memory updated across refinement rounds, with KernelBench evaluation and memory ablations.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 010; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Qitong Sun, Jun Han, Tianlin Li, Zhe Tang, Sheng Chen, Fei Yang, Aishan Liu, Xianglong Liu, Yang Liu
titulo: KernelSkill: A Multi-Agent Framework for GPU Kernel Optimization
formato_armazenamento: Dual-level memory: long-term expert skill memory with standardized profiling fields, code features, bottleneck rules, decision tables, and method knowledge; short-term repair and optimization trajectory memory recording plans, compiler/verifier/profiler feedback, and kernel versions.
framework_agente: KernelSkill multi-agent GPU kernel optimization framework with generator, compiler, verifier, profiler, diagnoser, repairer, planner, and optimizer roles
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; ablation; refinement-efficiency analysis; baseline comparison
benchmarks_utilizados: KernelBench Levels 1-3 with 250 tasks; NVIDIA A100 80GB; CUDA 12.4; cuDNN 9.1.0; cuBLAS 12.9
metricas_desempenho: success rate, Fast1, average runtime speedup over Torch Eager, per-round speedup, correctness verification, latency with warmup and timed CUDA events
metricas_cl: Long-term memory ablation, short-term memory ablation, no-memory ablation, cross-task reusable skill selection, per-task trajectory-state effect
baseline_comparado: Kevin-32B, Astra, PRAGMA, CudaForge, QiMeng, STARK, Torch Eager, and KernelSkill ablations
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Long-term schema stores expert optimization skills and decision rules; short-term memory is updated across up to 15 refinement rounds per task. Each task samples 3 seed kernels and promotes new base kernels only after relative or absolute speedup thresholds.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; experiments report hardware, candidate seeds, and up to 15 refinement rounds per task but not monetary cost.
resultado_principal: KernelSkill achieves 100% success on KernelBench Levels 1-3 and average speedups of 5.44x, 2.82x, and 1.92x over Torch Eager.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Compared with STARK, KernelSkill improves average speedup by 79.5% on Level 1, 4.8% on Level 2, and 21.5% on Level 3. It reaches these gains with 15 refinement rounds, while STARK reports 30 rounds.
custo_vs_baseline: KernelSkill reports stronger speedup per refinement round than STARK: 0.36/0.19/0.13 on Levels 1/2/3 versus 0.10/0.09/0.05 for STARK.
limitacoes: Some baselines are not open-sourced, so several comparisons use reported results or reimplementations rather than a single identical codebase; evaluation is limited to KernelBench GPU-kernel tasks.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on NVIDIA A100 hardware, profiling tools, seed selection, fixed refinement rounds, threshold settings, ChatGPT-5.1 behavior, and comparability of non-open-source baseline results.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes PhyloEvolve, a GPU code-optimization agent system with persistent phylogenetic trees and an elite pool that store trajectories, high-performing variants, reusable modifications, and failure patterns for cross-lineage reuse across scientific-code optimization tasks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 010; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Leyi Zhao, Weijie Huang, Yitong Guo, Jiang Bian, Chenghong Wang, Xuhong Zhang
titulo: Large Language Model-Powered Evolutionary Code Optimization on a Phylogenetic Tree
formato_armazenamento: Persistent phylogenetic forest whose nodes store algorithm versions, code, performance metrics, modification history, and rewards; global Elite Pool stores elite trajectories and reusable elite modifications with performance statistics.
framework_agente: PhyloEvolve multi-agent GPU scientific-code optimization framework with NextStepper, ModifyAgent, Designer, and Summarizer agents
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; controlled sandbox execution; trajectory-conditioned evolutionary optimization; qualitative and quantitative performance analysis
benchmarks_utilizados: Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation solver, Local Tangent Space Alignment manifold learning, GraphWave spectral graph embedding; NVIDIA A40 workstation with Docker CUDA execution
metricas_desempenho: wall-clock runtime, memory efficiency, correctness success/failure signal, scalar performance score, trajectory rewards, convergence behavior
metricas_cl: Elite trajectory reuse, elite modification catalog, cross-lineage transfer, pruning with informative failure retention, summarization of successful patterns
baseline_comparado: Baseline implementations and evolutionary search variants for the scientific-code optimization workloads
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Phylogenetic trees and a forest-level elite pool grow across optimization iterations, preserving complete or partial high-performing trajectories, modification histories, rewards, and reusable optimization motifs.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; experiments report GPU hardware, Docker execution, timeouts, memory limits, checkpointing, pruning, and up to three automatic debug retries.
resultado_principal: PhyloEvolve reports consistent performance-enhancing transformations across PDE solving, manifold learning, and spectral graph workloads by reusing optimization trajectories rather than relying only on mutation-driven evolutionary search.
desempenho_vs_baseline: The paper reports improvements in runtime performance, memory efficiency, and correctness over baseline and evolutionary methods, including reusable motifs such as kernel fusion, shared-memory tiling, and batching strategies.
custo_vs_baseline: No monetary or token-cost comparison is reported; the discussion emphasizes that trajectory-conditioned reuse reduces redundant exploration but still depends on empirical evaluation.
limitacoes: The system lacks hardware-aware cost models, can be inefficient for computationally heavy workloads, focuses on NVIDIA GPUs, does not address multi-GPU or distributed execution, and may produce occasional low-quality LLM-generated modifications.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on empirical runtime measurement, hardware/backend choice, benchmark workload selection, container execution, pruning settings, LLM proposal quality, and the absence of a theoretical account of when trajectory reuse yields stable improvement operators.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes LatentMem, a multi-agent memory framework with an experience bank storing raw historical trajectories and a learnable memory composer that retrieves and distills them for future tasks, evaluated on coding benchmarks including KodCode and BigCodeBench.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 010; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Muxin Fu, Guibin Zhang, Xiangyuan Xue, Yafu Li, Zefeng He, Siyuan Huang, Xiaoye Qu, Yu Cheng, Yang Yang
titulo: LatentMem: Customizing Latent Memory for Multi-Agent Systems
formato_armazenamento: Experience bank stores raw multi-agent trajectories in lightweight form; memory composer retrieves trajectories and synthesizes fixed-length role-aware latent memory tokens conditioned on agent context; LMPO trains the composer from task-level rewards.
framework_agente: LatentMem learnable multi-agent memory framework with experience bank, role profiles, memory composer, latent memory injection, and LMPO training
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; cross-domain generalization; cross-MAS generalization; ablation; sensitivity analysis; time and token cost analysis; fine-tuning comparison
benchmarks_utilizados: TriviaQA, KodCode, StrategyQA, PopQA, BigCodeBench, PDDL across AutoGen, MacNet, CAMEL, and DyLAN multi-agent frameworks
metricas_desempenho: task performance percentage, average improvement, held-in and held-out benchmark performance, time cost, token cost, latent memory length sensitivity, retrieval-count sensitivity
metricas_cl: Experience-bank real-time update ablation, role-aware memory ablation, 40,580 stored trajectories, latent token length L'=8, K retrieval sensitivity
baseline_comparado: No-memory, MetaGPT, Generative, Voyager, G-Memory, OAgent/OAgents, JoyAgent, ChatDev, MARTI fine-tuning baseline
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Training collection contains 40,580 trajectories from AutoGen and MacNet on TriviaQA, KodCode, StrategyQA, and PopQA. The chosen latent memory length is L'=8.
custo_por_tarefa: NR per task; figures report aggregate time and token consumption, for example LatentMem uses 1.09e4 seconds on MacNet+KodCode and 2.31e7 tokens on CAMEL+PopQA.
reducao_tokens: The paper reports about one-third fewer tokens and inference time reduced to about two-thirds relative to mainstream memory designs; it also reports lowest token usage among memory baselines in several plotted settings.
resultado_principal: LatentMem achieves up to 19.36% gain over vanilla settings and consistently outperforms existing memory architectures across diverse benchmarks and MAS frameworks.
desempenho_vs_baseline: In Table 4 with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, LatentMem improves AutoGen average from 52.86 to 61.82, MacNet from 53.38 to 61.00, CAMEL from 53.37 to 60.04, and DyLAN from 52.80 to 60.06; KodCode gains range from +14.90 to +18.45 points.
custo_vs_baseline: LatentMem is reported as fastest and lowest-token among memory baselines in the added cost figures; examples include 1.76e6 tokens on AutoGen+KodCode versus G-Memory at 3.04e6 and 1.09e4 seconds on MacNet+KodCode versus G-Memory at 2.02e4.
limitacoes: The paper emphasizes effectiveness and efficiency but still depends on collected in-domain trajectories, learned composer behavior, role-profile quality, and benchmark/MAS framework coverage; out-of-domain generalization is evaluated but not exhaustive.
ameacas_validade: Results may depend on synthetic coding and planning benchmarks, selected MAS frameworks, trajectory collection protocol, composer base model, LMPO reward design, latent-length and retrieval-count settings, and LLM-as-component implementation choices.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes Online Repository Memory that learns reusable skills from chronological historical commits and evaluates future pull-request generation on held-out later repository changes.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 010; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Mo Li, L. H. Xu, Qitai Tan, Ting Cao, Yunxin Liu
titulo: Learning to Commit: Generating Organic Pull Requests via Online Repository Memory
formato_armazenamento: Online Repository Memory represented as a continuously updated repository-specific skill document derived from chronological commits through blind attempt, oracle diff comparison, contrastive reflection, and CRUD skill updates.
framework_agente: Learning to Commit repository-adaptive coding agent with supervised contrastive reflection on historical commits
llm_utilizado: Claude Opus 4.6; Claude Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 Pro used as judges
metodo_avaliacao: chronological held-out future-task evaluation; deterministic code metrics; pairwise LLM judging; case study
benchmarks_utilizados: Internal expert-maintained reinforcement-learning training repository with 2,738 non-merge commits filtered to 386 substantive commits; 24 historical learning commits and 7 future held-out test tasks
metricas_desempenho: File IoU, trajectory steps, line deviation ratio, pairwise win rate, Q1 scope alignment, Q2 logic similarity, Q3 redundancy and hallucination, Q4 code style
metricas_cl: Skill-document updates through on-policy contrastive reflection; chronological temporal split; sequential versus parallel learning modes; all-corpus versus category-specific curricula
baseline_comparado: Base agent without skill document under the same Claude Opus 4.6 tool-use capabilities
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Pilot starts from 2,738 non-merge commits, filters to 386 substantive commits, and uses 24 historical learning commits to build memory before 7 future test tasks; diffs over 180K tokens are excluded.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; paper reports trajectory steps but not monetary or token cost.
resultado_principal: Skill-conditioned agents improve file localization, reduce patch bloat, and win more pairwise LLM-judge comparisons than the no-skill baseline in three of four settings under both judges.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Table 2 reports File IoU improvements in three of four settings, including 80% versus 61% in seq-all and 80% versus 68% in par-bycat. Table 3 reports skill-agent win rates of 54%/57% in par-bycat and 55%/58% in seq-all under Claude/Gemini judges.
custo_vs_baseline: The skill-conditioned agent uses fewer trajectory steps in seq-all (56.8 versus 71.9) and slightly fewer or similar steps in most settings, but no monetary cost is reported.
limitacoes: Evaluation is a pilot on one highly coupled expert-maintained repository with 7 future held-out tasks; LLM judges may introduce bias; public multi-repository generalization is left for future work.
ameacas_validade: Synthetic issue generation, LLM-based commit filtering and judging, small future-test sample, single repository domain, temporal split construction, and skill verbosity may influence the organicity results.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes MEMCoder using multi-dimensional evolving task-level and API-level memory that is updated from execution feedback and reused for private-library-oriented code generation on NdonnxEval and NumbaEval.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 011; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Mofei Li, Taozhi Chen, Guowei Yang, Jia Li
titulo: MEMCoder: Multi-dimensional Evolving Memory for Private-Library-Oriented Code Generation
formato_armazenamento: Multi-dimensional Evolving Memory with task-level entries storing requirements, generated code, execution feedback, used APIs, and cross-API usage guidelines; API-level entries storing API documentation, code snippets, execution outcomes, dynamic usage guidelines, and guideline weights.
framework_agente: MEMCoder private-library-oriented code-generation framework with guideline-driven generation and feedback-driven memory evolution
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; RAG baseline comparison; continual-learning baseline comparison; ablation; token-cost analysis
benchmarks_utilizados: NdonnxEval and NumbaEval private-library code-generation benchmarks
metricas_desempenho: Pass@1, Pass@3, Pass@5, Exec@1, Exec@3, Exec@5
metricas_cl: Task-level memory ablation, API-level memory ablation, naive accumulation ablation, guideline extraction/refinement, dynamic guideline weight update, long-term memory bloat discussion
baseline_comparado: Naive RAG, EpiGen, CAPIR, ReMem, DC-RS, MEMCoder ablations, and oracle static documentation analysis
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Memory grows sequentially from generated code, execution feedback, task-level usage guidelines, API-level usage guidelines, code snippets, and weights; task-level retrieval uses similar historical tasks and API-level retrieval uses candidate API memories.
custo_por_tarefa: On NumbaEval, MEMCoder consumes an average of 17,111 additional total tokens per task, with estimated extra cost of about USD 0.46 for the whole 187-task benchmark.
resultado_principal: MEMCoder yields an average absolute Pass@1 gain of 16.31% when integrated into static RAG baselines and outperforms existing memory-based continual-learning baselines.
desempenho_vs_baseline: With Qwen2.5-Coder, Naive RAG plus MEMCoder reaches 52.54 Pass@1 on NdonnxEval and 33.74 on NumbaEval versus Naive RAG at 27.22 and 23.16. In continual-learning comparison, MEMCoder reaches 33.74 Pass@1 on NumbaEval versus ReMem 23.16 and DC-RS 17.33.
custo_vs_baseline: MEMCoder adds token overhead for memory injection and guideline refinement compared with naive RAG, but the paper estimates the added full-NumbEval cost as about USD 0.46.
limitacoes: Additional memory injection and reflection introduce token overhead; long-term memory growth may cause retrieval inefficiency and knowledge conflicts; benchmark scope is private-library generation on NdonnxEval and NumbaEval.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on local open-source models, bge-base-en-prior.5 retrieval, task ordering, memory-guideline quality, execution feedback reliability, private-library benchmark representativeness, and memory conflict management.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes MemCollab, a shared cross-agent memory framework that distills reusable guidance from contrasted trajectories and retrieves it for future inference, with quantitative evaluation on code-generation benchmarks including MBPP and HumanEval.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 011; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Yurui Chang, Yiran Wu, Qingyun Wu, Lu Lin
titulo: MemCollab: Cross-Agent Memory Collaboration via Contrastive Trajectory Distillation
formato_armazenamento: Shared memory bank of contrast-derived normative constraints. Each entry summarizes an enforced reasoning invariant and an avoided violation pattern distilled from preferred and unpreferred trajectories generated by heterogeneous agents on the same task.
framework_agente: MemCollab collaborative memory framework with contrast-derived memory construction and task-aware retrieval
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; cross-agent transfer; cross-model-family transfer; reasoning-turn efficiency; retrieval ablation; case study
benchmarks_utilizados: MATH500, GSM8K, MBPP, HumanEval
metricas_desempenho: accuracy, average score, average reasoning turns, retrieval-count sensitivity, retrieval-strategy comparison
metricas_cl: Contrast-derived memory entries, shared agent-agnostic memory bank, task-category labels, top-p task-aware retrieval, comparison with self-memory and direct cross-model memory transfer
baseline_comparado: Vanilla agent, Buffer of Thoughts, Dynamic Cheatsheet, memory from same model, memory from another model, self-contrast memory
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Each task contributes up to K extracted memory entries; inference retrieves top-3 relevant samples within task-filtered subsets, with each retrieved sample contributing up to three memory entries.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; experiments report reasoning-turn reduction and use 8 NVIDIA RTX 6000 GPUs with CUDA 12.8, but no monetary cost.
resultado_principal: MemCollab improves both weaker and stronger agents by constructing shared memory from contrastive trajectories rather than directly transferring model-specific memory.
desempenho_vs_baseline: For Qwen2.5-7B, MemCollab improves average accuracy from 57.1 to 71.6; MBPP improves from 47.9 to 57.6 and HumanEval from 42.7 to 74.4. For Qwen2.5-32B, average accuracy improves from 70.8 to 79.6.
custo_vs_baseline: MemCollab reduces average reasoning turns for Qwen2.5-7B: MATH500 2.7 to 2.2, GSM8K 1.8 to 1.6, MBPP 3.1 to 1.4, and HumanEval 3.3 to 1.5.
limitacoes: Direct memory transfer between models can degrade performance; too many retrieved memory entries can reduce performance after a task-dependent threshold; evaluation spans math and code benchmarks rather than full software-engineering workflows.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on task classification, TF-IDF retrieval, top-3 memory budget, paired-agent choice, benchmark sample construction, random seed, and whether contrastive summaries suppress model-specific biases without dropping useful details.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes MemGovern transforming cross-repository human debugging experience into governed experience cards and an agentic experience-search memory for code agents, with SWE-bench Verified evaluation.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `wang2026memgovern` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_experience_bank. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Qihao Wang, Ziming Cheng, Shuo Zhang, Fan Liu, Rui Xu, Heng Lian, Kunyi Wang, Xiaoming Yu, Jianghao Yin, Sen Hu, Yue Hu, Shaolei Zhang, Yanbing Liu, Ronghao Chen, Huacan Wang
titulo: MemGovern: Enhancing Code Agents through Learning from Governed Human Experiences
formato_armazenamento: 135K experience cards em vector database. Cada card: Index Layer (Problem Summary normalizado + Diagnostic Signals generalizáveis como exception types, error signatures, component tags) + Resolution Layer (Root Cause, Fix Strategy abstrata, Patch Digest semântico, Verification steps). Pipeline de governança: Experience Selection (repo scoring) → Standardization (content purification + unified repair experience protocol) → Quality Control (LLM checklist scoring com refine loop).
framework_agente: swe_agent
llm_utilizado: Claude 4 Sonnet, GPT-5 Medium, DeepSeek-V3.1T, Qwen3-235B, Kimi-K2-Instruct, Qwen3-Coder-30B, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-Mini
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified (500)
metricas_desempenho: Resolved Rate (%), Avg. Tokens (M), Avg. Cost ($)
baseline_comparado: SWE-Agent (sem memória), AutoCodeRover, CodeAct, SWESynInfer, mini-SWE-agent, RAG estático, Agentic RAG
tokens_entrada_por_tarefa: 0.78M-2.35M tokens por tarefa (varia por LLM backbone)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Fixo após construção offline (135K cards); ganhos monotônicos de 10% a 100% do pool em ablação
custo_por_tarefa: $0.07 (Qwen3-Coder-30B) a $7.27 (Claude 4 Sonnet) por tarefa
reducao_tokens: NR (overhead leve: tokens aumentam ~3-5% vs baseline por conta de retrieval)
resultado_principal: MemGovern melhora SWE-Agent em média +4.65pp em resolve rate no SWE-bench Verified, consistente em 7 LLMs. Maiores ganhos em modelos fracos (+9.4pp GPT-4o, +8.2pp Qwen3-235B). Agentic Search (search-then-browse) supera RAG estático e Agentic RAG em todos os modelos testados.
desempenho_vs_baseline: +3.2pp com Claude 4 Sonnet (69.8% vs 66.6%); +8.2pp com Qwen3-235B (55.4% vs 47.2%); +9.4pp com GPT-4o (32.6% vs 23.2%); média +4.65pp em 7 LLMs
custo_vs_baseline: Overhead moderado: $6.94→$7.27 com Claude 4 Sonnet (+4.8%); $0.14→$0.20 com GPT-4o-Mini (+43%); proporcional ao uso de Searching/Browsing tools
limitacoes: Overhead de tokens adicionais para busca na memória experiencial; experience cards construídos offline via GPT-5.1 (custo de construção não reportado); estratégias de compressão de memória não exploradas; Index Layer remove especificidade de repositório
ameacas_validade: SWE-bench Verified como único benchmark; experience cards geradas por GPT-5.1 podem ter viés; 150K triplets GitHub pré-filtrados podem sobrepor temporalmente com SWE-bench; avaliação de quality control via LLM-judge; human evaluation limitada a 100 repositórios para seleção
