autores: Kangsan Kim, Minki Kang, Taeil Kim, Yanlai Yang, Mengye Ren, Sung Ju Hwang
titulo: Memory Transfer Learning: How Memories are Transferred Across Domains in Coding Agents
formato_armazenamento: Unified heterogeneous-domain memory pools storing four representations: Trajectory command/action histories, Workflow action sequences, Summary task and experience summaries, and Insight task-agnostic memory items with title, description, and content.
framework_agente: Memory Transfer Learning experimental coding-agent framework using mini-swe-agent, offline memory generation, cross-domain memory pools, and embedding retrieval
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; cross-domain transfer study; self-evolving baseline comparison; negative-transfer analysis; memory-abstraction analysis; memory-pool scaling; cross-model transfer; retrieval-method ablation
benchmarks_utilizados: LiveCodeBenchv6, Aider-Polyglot, SWE-Bench Verified, TerminalBench2, ReplicationBench, MLGym-Bench
metricas_desempenho: Pass@3, average score, Pass@1 in appendix/scaling/cross-model studies, memory count, memory-pool size, number of source domains
metricas_cl: Memory type comparison across Trajectory, Workflow, Summary, and Insight; cross-domain transfer; cross-model transfer; negative-transfer categories; abstraction and retrieval analysis
baseline_comparado: Zero-shot coding agent, ReasoningBank, AgentKB, memory retrieval variants with LLM reranking and adaptive rewriting
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: In the self-evolving comparison, MTL uses 431 memories versus ReasoningBank's 97 and AgentKB's 5,899. Inference retrieves three memories per query from the heterogeneous-domain pool.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: Cross-domain memory improves average performance by 3.7% for GPT-5-mini, with the strongest gains from high-level Insight memories rather than low-level trajectory memories.
desempenho_vs_baseline: With GPT-5-mini, zero-shot average Pass@3 is 0.523 and MTL with Insight reaches 0.560. MTL outperforms ReasoningBank and AgentKB on the three-benchmark comparison with average 0.630 versus 0.601 and 0.613.
custo_vs_baseline: MTL uses fewer stored memories than AgentKB in the self-evolving comparison (431 versus 5,899) while reaching a higher average score; no monetary or token-cost comparison is reported.
limitacoes: Memory transfer can cause negative transfer through domain-mismatched anchors, false validation confidence, and misapplied best-practice transfer; advanced static retrieval strategies underperform simple embedding similarity.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on sampled benchmark tasks, GPT-5-mini memory generation and judging, embedding retrieval, source-domain composition, memory format prompts, cross-model transfer setup, and whether benchmark domains represent real deployment diversity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes structured experience records generated after verification failures, accumulated and reused across later Python-to-C++ translation phases, with HumanEval-X evaluation and reported Pass@k improvements.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 011; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Kaiyuan Feng, Furong Peng, Jiayue Wu
titulo: ODEL: An Experience-Augmented Self-Evolving Framework for Efficient Python-to-C++ Code Translation
formato_armazenamento: Experience repository of structured corrective records generated after verification failures, including error patterns, corrective strategies, and applicability conditions; records are retrieved in later translation iterations to guide Qwen2.5-Coder generation.
framework_agente: ODEL on-demand experience-enhanced Python-to-C++ translation framework with logic interpreter, code generator, code validator, and teacher-student experience distillation
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; multi-phase longitudinal evaluation; experience-source ablation; cross-model translation evaluation
benchmarks_utilizados: HumanEval-X Python-to-C++ translation tasks with 164 programming problems
metricas_desempenho: Pass@1, Pass@10, phase-wise Pass@1 trend, comparative improvements
metricas_cl: Experience-source ablation, internal versus external experience generation, phase-wise accumulated-experience reuse, teacher-student experience distillation
baseline_comparado: Qwen2.5-Coder single-agent, UniTrans Method, Multi-Agent no-experience, ODEL internal experience, Deepseek-Coder single-agent, Deepseek-Coder ODEL
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Experience records accumulate across successive translation phases; the paper reports monotonic Pass@1 improvement over five consecutive ODEL external-experience trials but does not report memory-entry counts.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; the discussion claims deployment-efficient, parameter-free adaptation can reduce development cost but does not provide monetary or token quantities.
resultado_principal: ODEL External Experience improves HumanEval-X Python-to-C++ Pass@1 from 71.82% to 81.10% and Pass@10 from 74.30% to 89.02% over Qwen2.5-Coder single-agent.
desempenho_vs_baseline: ODEL External Experience outperforms UniTrans at 72.16% Pass@1 and 76.64% Pass@10. It also improves over ODEL Internal Experience by 6.35 Pass@1 points and 4.32 Pass@10 points.
custo_vs_baseline: No numeric cost comparison is reported; ODEL invokes the external DeepSeek 685B teacher only on verification failure, while routine translation remains with the internal model.
limitacoes: The multi-phase evaluation uses a fixed HumanEval-X task set and consistent task ordering; results reflect sustained learning under a stable stream rather than strict order-invariant generalization.
ameacas_validade: Findings depend on generated test cases, verification feedback quality, HumanEval-X representativeness, external teacher model quality, task ordering, and potential leakage concerns mitigated by storing abstract error patterns rather than task-specific solutions.

contextual_role: Human-authored persistent artifact evidence: repository AGENTS.md files and their measured impact on coding-agent efficiency.
full_text_rationale: Full text empirically studies persistent repository-level AGENTS.md instruction files in AI coding-agent runs over 124 GitHub pull-request tasks, measuring token use and wall-clock time; this fits the documented human-curated artifact exception because it evaluates impact on coding-agent performance.
extractor_notes: Contextual human-artifact exception: AGENTS.md is human-authored but empirically evaluated for coding-agent efficiency.
autores: Jai Lal Lulla, Seyedmoein Mohsenimofidi, Matthias Galster, Jie M. Zhang, Sebastian Baltes, Christoph Treude
titulo: On the Impact of AGENTS.md Files on the Efficiency of AI Coding Agents
formato_armazenamento: Repository-root AGENTS.md files as persistent, human-authored, version-controlled instruction artifacts for coding agents.
framework_agente: OpenAI Codex CLI
llm_utilizado: gpt-5.2-codex; gpt-oss-120b used for AGENTS.md file filtering/classification
benchmarks_utilizados: Paired evaluation on 10 repositories and 124 real GitHub pull-request tasks, with and without a root AGENTS.md file.
metricas_desempenho: Wall-clock time, input tokens, cached input tokens, output tokens, total tokens, task-completion behavior sanity check.
baseline_comparado: Same repository snapshots and tasks without AGENTS.md.
tokens_entrada_por_tarefa: Mean input tokens 353,010.01 without AGENTS.md vs 318,651.51 with AGENTS.md.
tokens_saida_por_tarefa: Mean output tokens 5,744.81 without AGENTS.md vs 4,591.46 with AGENTS.md.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: NA
custo_por_tarefa: Token usage reported; no monetary cost per task.
reducao_tokens: Mean output tokens reduced by 1,153.35 (20.08%); median output tokens reduced by 485 (16.58%); mean total tokens reduced by 68,310.43 (9.93%).
resultado_principal: Presence of AGENTS.md is associated with lower runtime and token use while maintaining comparable task-completion behavior. Mean wall-clock time falls from 162.94s to 129.91s and median time from 98.57s to 70.34s.
desempenho_vs_baseline: With AGENTS.md: mean runtime 20.27% lower, median runtime 28.64% lower, mean output tokens 20.08% lower, median output tokens 16.58% lower.
custo_vs_baseline: Mean total token usage decreases 9.93%; mean input and cached-input tokens decrease 9.73% and 9.97%, respectively.
limitacoes: Initial workshop study; single agent/model configuration; selected 10 repositories and 124 PR tasks; semantic correctness and alignment are not fully evaluated.
ameacas_validade: Paired design controls tasks and repositories but not generality across agents/models; task selection and AGENTS.md filtering may introduce sampling bias; output equivalence is only sanity checked.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes OpenGame's evolving Template Skill and Debug Skill, which accumulate reusable project skeletons and verified debugging protocols for web-game coding, with OpenGame-Bench evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 011; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Yilei Jiang, Jinyuan Hu, Qianyin Xiao, Yaozhi Zheng, Ruize Ma, Kaituo Feng, Jiaming Han, Tianshuo Peng, Kaixuan Fan, Manyuan Zhang, Xiangyu Yue
titulo: OpenGame: Open Agentic Coding for Games
formato_armazenamento: Game Skill with Template Skill and Debug Skill. Template Skill stores an evolving library of reusable project skeleton families; Debug Skill stores a living debugging protocol of error signatures, root causes, verified fixes, and pre-execution validations.
framework_agente: OpenGame web-game coding framework with Game Skill, Template Skill, Debug Skill, GameCoder-27B, and OpenGame-Bench
llm_utilizado: GameCoder-27B, Qwen-3.5-27B, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.1, Gemini 3.1 Pro, DeepSeek V3.2, Kimi K2.5, MiniMax m2.5, GLM-4.5
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; dynamic browser execution; VLM judging; model comparison; agent workflow ablation; game-skill maturity ablation; bounded debugging analysis; genre breakdown
benchmarks_utilizados: OpenGame-Bench with 150 natural-language web-game prompts across platformers, top-down shooters, puzzle games, arcade classics, and strategy
metricas_desempenho: Build Health, Visual Usability, Intent Alignment, valid-run means over three seeds, debugging-iteration sensitivity, genre-level Intent Alignment
metricas_cl: Template Skill maturity from meta-template to five template families; Debug Skill maturity from static checklist to post-execution fixes and full living protocol; bounded iterative debugging.
baseline_comparado: Direct LLMs, qwen-code, Cursor, Qwen-3.5-27B, GameCoder-27B, ablations without hook-driven implementation, three-layer reading, physics-first classification, evolved templates, and living debug protocol
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Template Skill evolves from one game-agnostic meta-template to five specialized families; Debug Skill grows with structured verified fixes and reusable inconsistency rules.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; the paper reports bounded automated debugging iterations but not token or monetary cost.
resultado_principal: OpenGame with Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves Build Health 72.4, Visual Usability 67.2, and Intent Alignment 65.1 on OpenGame-Bench.
desempenho_vs_baseline: OpenGame with Claude Sonnet 4.6 outperforms Cursor with Claude Sonnet 4.6 by 5.6 Build Health, 5.8 Visual Usability, and 6.2 Intent Alignment points. GameCoder-27B reaches 63.9/57.0/54.1 and outperforms every direct LLM baseline on Build Health and Intent Alignment.
custo_vs_baseline: No numeric cost comparison is reported; ablation shows bounded iterative debugging improves reliability and begins to plateau after three iterations.
limitacoes: Even the full OpenGame leaves about 34.9% of weighted mechanical requirements partially or fully unsatisfied; strategy and Puzzle/UI genres remain difficult because silent logic desynchronization may not trigger compiler or runtime errors.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on OpenGame-Bench prompt design, VLM judging, Phaser instruction constraints for baselines, valid-run filtering, browser execution, seed averaging, and whether web-game tasks generalize to other software domains or engines.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes OpenSage with AI-created graph-based long-term memory managed by a dedicated memory agent, sharing high-level knowledge across tasks and evaluated on SWE-Bench Pro and other agent benchmarks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 011; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Hongwei Li, Zhun Wang, Qinrun Dai, Yuzhou Nie, Jinjun Peng, Ruitong Liu, Jingyang Zhang, Kaijie Zhu, Jingxuan He, Lun Wang, Yangruibo Ding, Yueqi Chen, Wenbo Guo, Dawn Song
titulo: OpenSage: Self-programming Agent Generation Engine
formato_armazenamento: Hierarchical graph memory with execution-based short-term memory and target-level long-term memory in Neo4j. Short-term graph stores agent runs, events, tool calls, raw outputs, summaries, and cross-agent calls; long-term graph stores entities, code structures, user queries, answers, and relationships managed by a memory agent.
framework_agente: OpenSage agent development kit with AI-created topology, dynamic tools, hierarchical graph memory, and dedicated memory agent
llm_utilizado: GPT-5, GPT-5.3-Codex, Gemini 3 Flash, Gemini 3 Pro, GPT-5 Mini, Claude Opus 4.5, Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; leaderboard comparison; topology ablation; tooling ablation; memory ablation; cost-throughput comparison; LOCOMO appendix evaluation
benchmarks_utilizados: CyberGym, Terminal-Bench 2.0, SWE-Bench Pro Python, DevOps-Gym, LOCOMO appendix
metricas_desempenho: resolved rate, confidence intervals for Terminal-Bench, cost in Terminal-Bench collaboration analysis, memory-ablation resolved rate, LOCOMO LLM-as-judge scores
metricas_cl: Graph-based short-term and long-term memory, memory-agent storage/retrieval/update operations, NoMem and Mem0g ablations, examples of stored search results, code understanding, and environment errors.
baseline_comparado: Anthropic Agent, OpenHands, Droid, Simple Codex, Claude Code, SWE-agent, Agentless, Mem0g, NoMem, OpenSage feature ablations
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Memory grows as agents create graph nodes and edges for short-term execution events and long-term target knowledge; the paper does not report aggregate memory-entry counts.
custo_por_tarefa: NR; Terminal-Bench analysis reports a resolved-rate and cost tradeoff for large-small collaboration, but no per-task memory cost is isolated.
resultado_principal: SageAgent via OpenSage ranks first in the reported comparisons: 60.2% resolved on CyberGym, 78.4 +/- 2.2 on Terminal-Bench 2.0, 59.0% on SWE-Bench Pro, and 46.8% on DevOps-Gym.
desempenho_vs_baseline: On SWE-Bench Pro, SageAgent with Gemini 3 Flash reaches 59.0% versus SWE-agent 40.2% and Agentless 9.4%. In the memory ablation, OpenSage reaches 59.0 versus Mem0g 56.4 and NoMem 56.2.
custo_vs_baseline: The Terminal-Bench collaboration analysis reports that pairing Gemini 3 Pro for planning/review with GPT-5 Mini for execution matches GPT-5 performance while reducing cost compared with Gemini 3 Pro alone.
limitacoes: The paper notes that current SOTA models do not yet use OpenSage's advanced features consistently and may misuse them, indicating a model-capability gap for AI-centered agent construction.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on benchmark leaderboards, selected backbones, added task-specific code for SWE-Bench Pro, graph-memory schema/tool design, generated node types, memory-agent decisions, and whether ADK-level gains generalize beyond the evaluated environments.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes Outcome-Conditioned Reasoning Distillation transforming historical same-repository resolved issues and verified patches into reusable procedural guidance for software-issue repair, with SWE-Bench Lite evaluation and temporal-leakage controls.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 012; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Chenglin Li, Yisen Xu, Zehao Wang, Shin Hwei Tan, Tse-Hsun Chen
titulo: Outcome-Conditioned Reasoning Distillation for Resolving Software Issues
formato_armazenamento: Repository-specific historical bug dataset storing resolved issues, verified patches, code context, affected locations, timestamps, and outcome-conditioned stage-wise repair reasoning plans; accepted exemplars are injected as structured guidance into repair stages.
framework_agente: O-CRD inference-time repair augmentation with repository-level exemplar mining, Exemplar Guardian compatibility filtering, Backward Reasoning Distillation, and Agentless execution scaffold
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; leaderboard comparison; controlled scaffold comparison; difficulty-stratified analysis; Exemplar Guardian ablation; MCTS-style forward-search ablation; token and call overhead analysis
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-Bench Lite with 300 real GitHub issues from 13 repositories
metricas_desempenho: Pass@1, file localization accuracy, function localization accuracy, difficulty-group Pass@1, McNemar significance test, LLM calls per issue, total tokens per issue
metricas_cl: Temporal-leakage-controlled historical exemplar reuse, same-repository exemplar retrieval, Exemplar Guardian stability, top-1 versus top-3 exemplar similarity, BRD versus MCTS reasoning-plan generation
baseline_comparado: Agentless, SWE-Agent, OpenHands, AutoCodeRover, MoatlessTools, MASAI, AutoCodeRover-v2, SWE-Search, O-CRD without Exemplar Guardian, O-CRD with MCTS-style forward search
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: For each of the 13 SWE-Bench Lite repositories, O-CRD builds a historical exemplar dataset from resolved bugs with verified patches that occurred strictly before the target issue; the paper does not report total memory-entry counts.
custo_por_tarefa: BRD averages 24.7 LLM calls and 14.3k total tokens per issue in the overhead ablation; the MCTS-style baseline averages 240 calls and 35.4k total tokens per issue.
reducao_tokens: Compared with the MCTS-style forward-search baseline, BRD uses 9.71x fewer LLM calls and 2.47x fewer total tokens per issue.
resultado_principal: On SWE-Bench Lite, O-CRD reaches Pass@1 of 37.7% with GPT-4o, 40.0% with GPT-5, and 35.6% with DeepSeek-V3.
desempenho_vs_baseline: With GPT-4o, O-CRD improves over Agentless from 27.3% to 37.7% Pass@1 and exceeds SWE-Search by 6.7 percentage points. With GPT-5, it improves Agentless from 29.7% to 40.0%; with DeepSeek-V3, from 27.0% to 35.6%.
custo_vs_baseline: In the reasoning-plan ablation, O-CRD with BRD achieves higher Pass@1 than the MCTS-style variant while using substantially fewer LLM calls and tokens.
limitacoes: The evaluation is limited to SWE-Bench Lite and depends on same-repository historical fixes; exemplar mismatch can cause negative transfer, and the cost comparison is against a controlled MCTS-style baseline rather than every possible search system.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on temporal filtering of historical issues, quality of verified patches, Agentless integration choices, LLM compatibility judgments, SWE-Bench Lite representativeness, and whether similar historical repairs exist for future repositories.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes Reasoning Memory, an external procedural-knowledge datastore distilled from prior reasoning trajectories and retrieved at test time, with quantitative evaluation across math, science, and coding benchmarks including LiveCodeBench.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 012; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Di Wu, Devendra Singh Sachan, Wen-tau Yih, Mingda Chen
titulo: Procedural Knowledge at Scale Improves Reasoning
formato_armazenamento: Reasoning Memory datastore of approximately 32 million compact procedural entries, each pairing a self-contained subquestion with a concise subroutine distilled from prior step-by-step reasoning trajectories.
framework_agente: Reasoning Memory retrieval-augmented reasoning framework with trajectory decomposition, self-verbalized in-thought query generation, dense retrieval, and test-time scaling over retrieved subroutines
llm_utilizado: DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B, OpenThinker3-7B, Qwen3-32B, QwQ-32B, Qwen3-8B, ReasonIR-8B retriever
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; retrieval-baseline comparison; compute-matched test-time scaling comparison; inference-budget scaling; datastore size and composition ablation; datastore/query-design ablation; qualitative failure analysis
benchmarks_utilizados: AIME 2024, AIME 2025, MATH500, GPQA-Diamond, LiveCodeBench V1-4, LiveCodeBench V5-6
metricas_desempenho: Accuracy, execution-based Pass@1 for LiveCodeBench, sample budget m, retained sample budget n, statistical significance markers
metricas_cl: Datastore size and composition effects, cross-domain procedural-transfer analysis, subquestion decomposition ablation, self-verbalized query ablation, inference-budget scaling to m=100
baseline_comparado: No RAG, Trajectory RAG prefix, Trajectory RAG summary, Template RAG ReasonFlux, Template RAG Self-Discover, Document RAG CompactDS, Document RAG Google, retrieval-free Length Scaling
tokens_saida_por_tarefa: Maximum output length is set to 32,768 tokens in the implementation; per-task input/output token means are not reported.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Final datastore contains approximately 32 million subquestion-subroutine pairs; average subquestions contain 19.2 tokens, average subroutines contain 207.9 tokens, and each source trajectory contributes 10.5 subquestions.
custo_por_tarefa: NR monetary; inference cost is reported through sample budgets such as m=8, m=30, and scaling analyses up to m=100.
resultado_principal: Across math, science, and coding benchmarks, Reasoning Memory improves over no retrieval by up to 19.2% and over the strongest compute-matched baseline by 7.9%.
desempenho_vs_baseline: On LiveCodeBench V1-4 with Qwen3-32B and m=30, Reasoning Memory reaches 0.471 versus Length Scaling 0.405 and No RAG 0.387 at m=8; on LiveCodeBench V5-6 it reaches 0.508 versus Length Scaling 0.476 and No RAG 0.465.
custo_vs_baseline: The method can outperform retrieval-free Length Scaling at matched sample budgets, but the paper does not provide monetary or end-to-end token-cost comparisons.
limitacoes: Document retrieval can degrade reasoning models; the method can fail when self-verbalized queries become over-specific or when retrieved procedures mismatch the problem setup; benefits vary after similar corpus knowledge is internalized by model training.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on Nemotron-derived trajectory coverage, decomposition prompt quality, ReasonIR retrieval, benchmark contamination assumptions, sample-budget choices, and whether math/science/coding reasoning transfers to agentic software-engineering workflows.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes ReCreate, an experience-driven framework that stores and retrieves task-agent trajectories, scaffold updates, tools, and static memory entries across iterations, with evaluation on SWE-Bench Verified, DA-Code, DS-1000, AppWorld, and other benchmarks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 012; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Zhezheng Hao, Hong Wang, Jian Luo, Jianqing Zhang, Yuyan Zhou, Qiang Lin, Can Wang, Hande Dong, Jiawei Chen
titulo: ReCreate: Reasoning and Creating Domain Agents Driven by Experience
formato_armazenamento: ReCreate-Environment stores current scaffold, full interaction trajectories, submitted artifacts, execution and evaluation results, codebase/database/sandbox context, evidence indexes, scaffold diffs, reusable tools, and static memory entries with lessons, failure modes, repair heuristics, and tool-use tips.
framework_agente: ReCreate bi-level domain-agent creation framework with a task agent, ReCreate-Agent scaffold optimizer, evidence retriever, creation router, hierarchical domain update, static memory bank, and tool creation
llm_utilizado: gpt-5-mini for task agents; claude-4.5-opus for ReCreate-Agent scaffold updates
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; multi-domain comparison; scaffold-generation baseline comparison; action-level ablation; reasoning-level ablation; cost comparison; temperature sensitivity; case study
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-Bench Verified Django and SymPy subsets, DA-Code data wrangling, DA-Code machine learning, DA-Code statistical analysis, DS-1000 NumPy, DS-1000 Pandas, DS-1000 Matplotlib, MATH Algebra, MATH Number Theory, MATH Counting and Probability, AppWorld Normal, AppWorld Challenge
metricas_desempenho: Pass rate or testing score, domain average score, action distribution, ablation scores, optimization cost in USD, temperature-sensitivity performance
metricas_cl: Iterative scaffold evolution over up to two optimization loops, development/test split, static memory accumulation, generated tools, domain-level update aggregation, cost of scaffold optimization across domains
baseline_comparado: CoT, Step-Back Abstraction, Self-Refine, LIVE, OPRO, ExpeL, ADAS, AgentSquare, ReCreate without creation router, ReCreate without domain update
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: The Django case study shows ReCreate evolving a minimal seed scaffold into prompt templates, memory interfaces, static memory entries, and custom tools; the paper does not report total memory-entry counts over all domains.
custo_por_tarefa: Figure 6 reports scaffold-optimization cost by domain; ReCreate reduces cost relative to ADAS by roughly 36% to 82%.
resultado_principal: Across 13 benchmark subsets, ReCreate obtains an average score of 66.15, higher than the strongest listed competing averages such as ExpeL at 60.62 and LIVE at 60.57.
desempenho_vs_baseline: On SWE subsets, ReCreate reaches 60.19 on Django and 63.64 on SymPy, exceeding the best baseline in each row. It also improves over ADAS and AgentSquare averages by more than seven points.
custo_vs_baseline: Compared with ADAS, ReCreate is reported to reduce scaffold-optimization cost by roughly 36% to 82% because it uses interaction evidence and smaller development sets rather than repeated candidate evaluation.
limitacoes: ReCreate optimizes textual and code-level scaffolds but not infrastructure or execution environments, and it does not update base model parameters.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on small sampled development sets, benchmark-specific scaffold design spaces, use of gpt-5-mini and claude-4.5-opus, Docker sandbox execution, cost-estimation assumptions, and whether domain-agent creation from minimal seeds generalizes beyond the tested domains.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes REVERE accumulating reusable heuristics, reflection history, and a persistent cheatsheet in a Global Training Context across research-coding tasks, with SUPER, ResearchCodeBench, and ScienceAgentBench evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 012; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Balaji Dinesh Gangireddi, Aniketh Garikaparthi, Manasi S. Patwardhan, Arman Cohan
titulo: REVERE: Reflective Evolving Research Engineer for Scientific Workflows
formato_armazenamento: Global Training Context containing auxiliary context, reflection history, and a cumulative cheatsheet; the Reflector edits system prompt, task prompt, and cheatsheet fields using targeted code-based updates across research-coding tasks.
framework_agente: REVERE reflective evolving research engineer with configurable prompt fields, Global Training Context, Reflector Agent, cheatsheet memory, and targeted edit mechanism
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; offline adaptation; online adaptation; prompt-adaptation baseline comparison; component ablation; efficiency and prompt-growth analysis; failure-mode case study
benchmarks_utilizados: SUPER Expert with 45 tasks, ResearchCodeBench with 212 tasks, ScienceAgentBench with 102 tasks
metricas_desempenho: SUPER Landmarks, SUPER Output Match, SUPER Overall, ResearchCodeBench Accuracy, ScienceAgentBench SuccessRate, ScienceAgentBench CodeBERTScore, adaptation cost in USD, prompt token length
metricas_cl: Offline and online adaptation over task sequences, Global Training Context ablation, cheatsheet ablation, reflection-history ablation, auxiliary-context ablation, prompt-component growth across iterations
baseline_comparado: Minimal baseline prompts, Static SOTA prompts, GEPA, ACE, REVERE without cheatsheet, REVERE without auxiliary context, REVERE without reflection history, REVERE without Global Training Context
tokens_entrada_por_tarefa: The paper reports reflector inputs of about 300k-500k tokens and agent inputs of about 40k-120k tokens in the research-coding settings; per-task means are not separately tabulated.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: The Global Training Context grows across adaptation iterations through reflection summaries and a cumulative cheatsheet; prompt-growth figures show controlled component growth but no exact memory-entry count.
custo_por_tarefa: Benchmark cost estimates are $1-3 per SUPER task, about $0.1-0.3 per ResearchCodeBench task, and about $0.2-0.4 per ScienceAgentBench task; REVERE adaptation cost is reported as nearly 10x lower than ACE and GEPA on SUPER.
resultado_principal: In offline adaptation with ground-truth hints, REVERE reaches SUPER Overall 29.8, ResearchCodeBench Accuracy 33.2, and ScienceAgentBench SuccessRate 28.39.
desempenho_vs_baseline: REVERE improves SUPER Overall by +4.50 over Static SOTA, ResearchCodeBench Accuracy by +1.3, and ScienceAgentBench SuccessRate by +4.89; removing the full Global Training Context drops the representative SUPER Overall from 34.7 to 16.0.
custo_vs_baseline: REVERE is reported to be up to about 10x more cost-effective than ACE and GEPA in adaptation overhead, using targeted edits and Global Training Context rather than refinement and Pareto selection.
limitacoes: Prompt and Global Training Context growth remain a challenge; REVERE still shows degraded CodeBERTScore on ScienceAgentBench relative to Static SOTA, and the paper notes the difficulty of capturing highly task-specific knowledge through prompt benchmarks.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on GPT-4.1's long context window, benchmark split choices, use of optional ground-truth hints in offline adaptation, estimated cost accounting, and whether research-coding heuristics transfer to broader software-engineering tasks.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes RuleFlow using an LLM-assisted optimization-discovery agent to convert discovered code optimizations into generalized reusable rewrite rules that accumulate over time and are applied to unseen programs, with PandasBench evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 012; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Avaljot Singh, Dushyant Bharadwaj, Stefanos Baziotis, Kaushik Varadharajan, Charith Mendis
titulo: RuleFlow: Generating Reusable Program Optimizations with LLMs
formato_armazenamento: Reusable Pandas rewrite rules represented in a domain-specific language with left-hand-side pattern, right-hand-side optimized code, and runtime preconditions; offline LLM-discovered optimizations are distilled into a validated rule corpus.
framework_agente: RuleFlow three-stage Pandas optimization framework with SnippetGen optimization discovery, RuleGen multi-agent rule synthesis, and CodeGen deterministic rewrite deployment
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; compiler/system baseline comparison; rewrite-rule speedup analysis; notebook-wise and rule-wise hit-rate analysis; yield analysis; qualitative invalid-rule analysis; multi-agent versus single-agent rule-generation comparison
benchmarks_utilizados: PandasBench with 102 real-world Python notebooks using the Pandas API
metricas_desempenho: Executed notebooks, mean speedup, median speedup, maximum speedup, minimum speedup, rule-hit counts, accepted optimization yield, valid-rule rate
metricas_cl: Offline accumulation of accepted optimization pairs, conversion to reusable rules, rule reuse on unseen notebooks, no LLM calls during deployment, multi-agent rule-generation validity comparison
baseline_comparado: DIAS, MODIN, DASK, KOALAS, single-agent RuleGen ablation
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: SnippetGen accepts 157 optimization pairs after filtering; RuleGen converts 120 optimization pairs into rewrite rules and the final evaluation uses 88 manually validated rules. Of these, 24 hit at least one notebook and 17 are scheduled by CodeGen.
custo_por_tarefa: NR monetary; LLM use is offline in discovery and rule generation, while deployment applies rewrite rules without further LLM calls.
resultado_principal: RuleFlow executes 101 of 102 PandasBench notebooks and achieves mean speedups of 1.54x over DIAS, 112.79x over MODIN, 12.32x over DASK, and 140.15x over KOALAS.
desempenho_vs_baseline: RuleFlow reaches a maximum speedup of 4.3x over DIAS and 1914.89x over MODIN; compared with a single-agent RuleGen variant, the multi-agent RuleGen obtains 68% valid rules versus 18%.
custo_vs_baseline: RuleFlow avoids per-program LLM calls at deployment by amortizing offline LLM discovery into reusable compiler rules, but it does not report monetary or token costs for discovery.
limitacoes: LLM-generated rules can be syntactically invalid, over-fitted, semantically divergent with null values, or unsafe under data-flow dependencies; the paper does not provide formal correctness guarantees for rewrite rules.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on PandasBench representativeness, default unscaled notebook workloads, qualitative manual rule filtering, runtime preconditions, Pandas data-corner cases, and whether reusable rewrite rules generalize beyond Pandas optimization.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes LLMA-Mem as a lifelong memory framework for multi-agent systems that distills procedural memories from episodic and transactive experience, supports cross-task reuse, and is evaluated on MultiAgentBench coding tasks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 012; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Shanglin Wu, Yuyang Luo, Yueqing Liang, Kaiwen Shi, Yanfang Ye, Ali Payani, Kai Shu
titulo: Scaling Teams or Scaling Time? Memory Enabled Lifelong Learning in LLM Multi-Agent Systems
formato_armazenamento: LLMA-Mem maintains episodic memory with task trajectories and outcomes, procedural memory with generalized strategies and success/failure counts, and transactive memory with agent profiles, team patterns, specialization, collaboration history, and success-rate estimates under local, shared, or hybrid topologies.
framework_agente: LLMA-Mem lifelong multi-agent memory framework integrated with MultiAgentBench collaborative environments, graph-based coordination, relevance-importance retrieval, episodic update, transactive update, and periodic procedural consolidation
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; lifelong sequence evaluation; model-family comparison; no-memory and memory-baseline comparison; cumulative moving average analysis; token-usage comparison; team-size scaling study; topology and consolidation-interval ablations
benchmarks_utilizados: MultiAgentBench collaborative coding, research, and database environments with 100 tasks per environment
metricas_desempenho: Task Score, Communication Score, Average of AS, cumulative moving average, cumulative memory advantage, average token usage per task
metricas_cl: Episodic-to-procedural consolidation every N tasks, success/failure counts for procedures, local/shared/hybrid memory topology comparison, team-size scaling from 1 to 7 agents, token-efficiency analysis over task sequences
baseline_comparado: No memory, MARBLE memory-enabled MultiAgentBench baseline, A-Mem dynamic memory framework, LLMA-Mem topology variants, LLMA-Mem consolidation-interval variants
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: The default setup retrieves top-3 memory items with maximum memory context 5 and consolidation interval N=5; memories persist as JSON files across tasks in the same sequential run.
custo_por_tarefa: Average token usage per task is compared across memory baselines; LLMA-Mem reduces token usage relative to MARBLE and A-Mem by 9.4% to 71.7% depending on model and environment.
reducao_tokens: Token reductions include 42.2% for Claude-Sonnet-4.5 on coding, 47.5% for DeepSeek-V3.2 on coding, 71.7% for Qwen3-32B-Instruct on research, 44.2% for Qwen3-32B-Instruct on database, 53.2% for Qwen3-next-80B on coding, 9.4% for DeepSeek-V3.2 on database, and 14.5% for Qwen3-next-80B on database.
resultado_principal: LLMA-Mem consistently improves Average of AS over the no-memory baseline in most model-environment pairs, with reported gains such as +5.92 for DeepSeek-V3.2 on research and +3.19 for Qwen3-32B-Instruct on database.
desempenho_vs_baseline: In the coding topology ablation with Qwen3-32B-Instruct, local memory obtains TS 49.68, CS 46.69, and AAS 49.43 versus shared 46.79/45.96/48.62 and hybrid 46.52/40.32/46.92.
custo_vs_baseline: LLMA-Mem lowers average token usage relative to MARBLE and A-Mem while improving long-horizon performance, showing that gains are not obtained by simply spending more context tokens.
limitacoes: The Qwen3-next-80B research setting shows reduced AAS despite higher task score, larger teams can incur coordination overhead and non-monotonic performance, and the study does not directly measure memory redundancy, staleness, or retrieval robustness.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on MultiAgentBench collaborative tasks, Claude-Sonnet-4.5 evaluation scoring, Amazon Bedrock inference settings, Titan embedding retrieval, chosen consolidation intervals, and whether coding/research/database environments represent broader multi-agent software work.

contextual_role: Benchmark evidence for self-evolution through knowledge internalization on coding tasks.
full_text_rationale: Full text proposes SE-Bench, a benchmark and empirical analysis for self-evolution through knowledge internalization on coding tasks, including memory-based baselines and quantitative evaluation of whether agents can reuse learned API knowledge without documentation.
extractor_notes: Contextual benchmark; evaluates internalization and memory/self-evolution baselines rather than proposing one deployed persistence architecture.
autores: Jiarui Yuan, Tailin Jin, Weize Chen, Zeyuan Liu, Zhiyuan Liu, Maosong Sun
titulo: SE-Bench: Benchmarking Self-Evolution with Knowledge Internalization
formato_armazenamento: NA
framework_agente: Self-evolution / knowledge-internalization training and memory baselines
benchmarks_utilizados: SE-Bench using obfuscated NumPy/ZWC package tasks with Single-Function and Multi-Function splits.
metricas_desempenho: Accuracy over five rollouts on single-function and multi-function tasks.
metricas_cl: Knowledge internalization under open-book/closed-book SFT, RL, self-play, and memory-based methods.
baseline_comparado: ACE, Expel, Open-SFT, Closed-SFT, Open-RL, Closed-RL, Absolute-Zero, Closed-SFT-RL across Qwen3 model sizes.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: NA
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: SE-Bench isolates knowledge internalization for self-evolving agents. Memory-based methods achieve non-trivial but imperfect results; Closed-SFT-RL is strongest overall, while standard RL methods fail completely in the reported table.
desempenho_vs_baseline: For Qwen3-8B, Closed-SFT-RL reaches 54.4% Single and 17.9% Multiple accuracy; Expel reaches 47.1% Single and 15.5% Multiple; Open/Closed RL and Absolute-Zero are 0.0%.
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Diagnostic benchmark built around obfuscated NumPy/ZWC coding tasks; may not represent broader software-engineering memory/persistence settings.
ameacas_validade: Synthetic obfuscation design, model-family focus on Qwen3, and benchmark-specific training/evaluation choices may limit generalization.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes SEVerA, a self-evolving verified-agent synthesis framework that maintains verified candidate agent programs and reuses execution traces/error diagnostics from training examples to synthesize improved agentic code, with evaluation on LLM-assisted Dafny program-verification benchmarks and other constrained tasks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 013; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Debangshu Banerjee, Changming Xu, Eugene Ie, Ming Zhang, Daiyi Peng, Chu-Cheng Lin, Gagandeep Singh
titulo: SEVerA: Verified Self-Evolving Agents
formato_armazenamento: Pool of verified candidate agent programs, formal output contracts, execution traces, error diagnostics, verifier feedback, learned parameters, and soft-objective optimization evidence used to synthesize and improve later agent programs.
framework_agente: SEVerA Formally Guarded Generative Model pipeline with planner, verifier, learning loop, and verified candidate archive
llm_utilizado: Claude Sonnet 4.5 for Dafny tasks; Qwen3-8B for tau2-bench and GSM-Symbolic learning experiments
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; formal-verification pass-rate comparison; constrained-task benchmark; ablation/overhead analysis
benchmarks_utilizados: HumanEvalDafny, DafnyBench, GSM-Symbolic, tau2-bench airline, tau2-bench retail
metricas_desempenho: Verification success, NoDiff rate, task accuracy/pass rate, constraint violations, wall-clock overhead per instance
metricas_cl: Iterative candidate synthesis and learning under hard formal constraints, with execution traces and verifier/error feedback reused by the planner.
baseline_comparado: LLM baselines, constrained decoding methods, Agent-C, and SEVerA ablations over verification/learning components
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Maintains verified candidates and feedback traces across synthesis iterations; the paper does not report total archive size over time.
custo_por_tarefa: NR monetary cost; wall-clock overhead is reported.
resultado_principal: SEVerA reports zero constraint violations across evaluated tasks, 97.0% verification plus NoDiff on HumanEvalDafny, 89.1% on DafnyBench, 66.0% accuracy on GSM-Symbolic, and 52.6% airline / 53.6% retail pass rates on tau2-bench.
desempenho_vs_baseline: SEVerA exceeds the best constrained-decoding method on GSM-Symbolic by 21.3 percentage points and reports 52.6% on tau2-bench airline versus 47.3% for Agent-C with Claude Sonnet 4.5.
custo_vs_baseline: Full SEVerA pipeline takes 18.2 seconds per HumanEvalDafny instance versus 9.8 seconds for an LLM baseline, and 25.6 seconds versus 10.3 seconds on DafnyBench.
limitacoes: Resource usage is not part of the formal constraints; the framework does not account for LLM calls, token budgets, or wall-clock budgets in synthesized agents.
ameacas_validade: Results depend on formal specification quality, verifier coverage, task selection, chosen LLM backbones, bounded candidate budget, and the representativeness of constrained verification and tau2-bench tasks.

full_text_rationale: Full text proposes SelfEvolve, a runtime code-generation architecture with a persistent knowledge base, functions repository, tool descriptors, and automated code reuse; accepted extensions are integrated for future requests and evaluated across 11 software self-extension tasks with baselines and ablation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 013; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Md Asif Iqbal Fahim, Oluwadamilola Adebayo, Alessio Ferrari
titulo: Software Self-Extension with SelfEvolve: an Agentic Architecture for Runtime Code Generation
formato_armazenamento: Persistent knowledge base with a Prompts repository, executable Functions repository, JSON Tool Descriptors repository, accepted generated function code, generated tests, feedback from rejected iterations, and invocation-pattern updates.
framework_agente: SelfEvolve runtime self-extension architecture with Chat and Tool Dispatcher, high-level-to-low-level requirement generation, TDD code generation, intermediate adjudicator, and hot reloading
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; repeated-run baseline comparison; TDD ablation; category-level analysis
benchmarks_utilizados: 11 manually created software self-extension tasks across integration, compositional, and data-processing categories, each run five times.
metricas_desempenho: Pass@1, successful runs out of 55, average iterations, task-category pass rates
metricas_cl: Accepted functions are written to the Functions repository and tool descriptors/prompts are updated so later requests can reuse newly generated capabilities.
baseline_comparado: AgentCoder, AutoGen, MetaGPT, and SelfEvolve without TDD
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: The paper reports accepted generated functions, descriptors, and prompt updates being accumulated in the knowledge base but does not report total repository growth over all runs.
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: SelfEvolve succeeds on 51 of 55 runs, or 92.7% Pass@1, across the 11 self-extension tasks.
desempenho_vs_baseline: SelfEvolve reaches 92.7% Pass@1 versus AutoGen at 30.9%, MetaGPT at 27.3%, and AgentCoder at 0.0%. Removing TDD reduces performance to 72.7% and raises average iterations from 2.2 to 4.7.
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Evaluation uses only 11 manually designed tasks, one LLM/function-calling stack, and functional-correctness metrics; it does not establish regression guarantees for old functions or broader performance/security/maintainability properties.
ameacas_validade: Potential test-code collusion because the same LLM generates tests and code, small benchmark size, manually created tasks, OpenAI-specific function-calling interface, and limited evidence for multi-function or long-running deployment settings.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes structurally aligned subtask-level memory that stores, retrieves, and incrementally updates reusable SWE-agent experience at subtask granularity, evaluated on SWE-bench Verified.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `shen2026structurally` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_experience_bank. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Kangning Shen, Jingyuan Zhang, Chenxi Sun, Wencong Zeng, Yang Yue
titulo: Structurally Aligned Subtask-Level Memory for Software Engineering Agents
formato_armazenamento: Memory state S_sub como repositório de subtask entries estruturadas: cada entry m = (z, d, e) onde z = categoria funcional (ANALYZE, REPRODUCE, EDIT, VERIFY), d = descrição estruturada (objective + mechanism keywords), e = experiência abstrata transferível extraída pelo LLM Extractor. Indexado por embeddings; atualizado online após cada subtask completion.
framework_agente: swe_agent
llm_utilizado: Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Claude 4.0 Sonnet
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified (500)
metricas_desempenho: Pass@1 (Avg ± std over 3 runs, Best@3)
baseline_comparado: Vanilla Agent (Mini SWE Agent sem memória), Instance-level Memory (reprodução de ReasoningBank)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Cresce monotonicamente durante streaming protocol (inicializa vazio); 797 entradas únicas após 500 instâncias; distribuição long-tail (top-100 = 33.8% dos retrievals, 50.7% single-use)
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: Subtask-level memory melhora Pass@1 em +4.7pp em média sobre vanilla agents em 4 LLMs no SWE-bench Verified. Ganhos são maiores em tarefas Hard (+8.7pp, >28 steps) e crescem com experiências acumuladas. Category isolation é crítica: global retrieval perde +2.3pp.
desempenho_vs_baseline: +6.8pp Gemini 2.5 Pro (60.3% vs 53.5%); +5.6pp Gemini 2.5 Flash (41.9% vs 36.3%); +3.9pp Claude 3.7 Sonnet (56.1% vs 52.2%); +2.3pp Claude 4.0 Sonnet (65.8% vs 63.5%); média +4.7pp
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Avaliação apenas no SWE-bench Verified; memória reiniciada a cada round experimental (não testa acumulação contínua real); extração de experiências pelo mesmo LLM backbone pode limitar qualidade; top-1 retrieval pode perder experiências relevantes
ameacas_validade: SWE-bench Verified predominantemente Python; memória reiniciada prejudica avaliação de escalabilidade long-term; 4 modelos testados mas apenas famílias Google e Anthropic; Instance-level Memory é reprodução de ReasoningBank, não implementação original; variância entre runs (σ até 2.27)

full_text_rationale: Full text describes SuperLocalMemory as a persistent cross-session local memory system for AI coding agents, including code-graph support, lifecycle hooks, forgetting/consolidation, and retrieval/memory benchmarks.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 013; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Varun Pratap Bhardwaj
titulo: SuperLocalMemory V3.3: The Living Brain -- Biologically-Inspired Forgetting, Cognitive Quantization, and Multi-Channel Retrieval for Zero-LLM Agent Memory Systems
formato_armazenamento: Local SQLite/sqlite-vec databases including memory.db, learning.db, and code_graph.db; cognitive memory taxonomy; code knowledge graph; lifecycle metadata; Ebbinghaus-style forgetting states; quantized memory tiers; and soft-prompt parameterization artifacts.
framework_agente: SuperLocalMemory V3.3 local-first memory system with MCP server, CLI, dashboard, Claude Code hooks, FRQAD retrieval, seven retrieval channels, adaptive forgetting, and code graph
llm_utilizado: Zero-LLM Mode A evaluation; designed for Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot Chat, Windsurf, MCP-compatible agents, and optional LLM modes
metodo_avaliacao: retrieval benchmark; LoCoMo benchmark; system benchmark; ablation and regression comparison
benchmarks_utilizados: FRQAD retrieval evaluation, LoCoMo Mode A and Mode C memory benchmark, system/package benchmarks
metricas_desempenho: Precision, LoCoMo accuracy, category-level gains, downloads, local CPU/system behavior
metricas_cl: Cross-session memory persistence, lifecycle hooks, forgetting/consolidation, hot/warm/cold/archive quantization, multi-channel retrieval, and code-graph persistence.
baseline_comparado: Cosine retrieval baseline, SuperLocalMemory V3.2 baseline, LoCoMo baselines and Mode C variants
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Memory is retained locally across sessions in SQLite databases and demoted from Active 32-bit to Warm 8-bit, Cold 4-bit, and Archive 2-bit tiers; the paper does not report a single longitudinal entry-count curve.
custo_por_tarefa: Zero-LLM Mode A runs locally without cloud API calls; no monetary per-task cost is reported.
resultado_principal: FRQAD plus mixed-precision retrieval reports 100% precision versus 85.6% for cosine retrieval. On LoCoMo Mode A, SuperLocalMemory V3.3 reports 70.4% zero-LLM accuracy with larger gains on multi-hop, temporal, and adversarial categories.
desempenho_vs_baseline: The paper reports +23.8 percentage points on multi-hop, +15.3 on temporal, and +12.7 on adversarial questions versus the V3.3 baseline, while V3.3 trails V3.2 overall LoCoMo Mode A by 4.4 percentage points due to fusion complexity.
custo_vs_baseline: Zero-LLM Mode A avoids LLM/API cost and runs on local CPU resources, but the paper does not isolate a comparable monetary-cost baseline.
limitacoes: Fusion complexity hurts simple/single-hop retrieval, fixed channel weights create cold-start sensitivity, and zero-friction lifecycle hooks are Claude Code-specific.
ameacas_validade: Single-system authorship, local benchmark design, LoCoMo mode choices, retrieval-channel weights, platform-specific hooks, and comparison to the prior V3.2 system may limit generalization.

contextual_role: Benchmark evidence for context/experience reuse across related repository-level coding tasks.
full_text_rationale: Full text describes SWE-ContextBench as a benchmark explicitly evaluating programming agents' ability to accumulate, retrieve, and reuse prior context/experience across related coding tasks.
extractor_notes: Contextual benchmark; includes memory/retrieval variants but main contribution is benchmark and measurement.
autores: Jared Zhu, Minhao Hu, Junde Wu
titulo: SWE Context Bench: A Benchmark for Context Learning in Coding
formato_armazenamento: Experience pool of prior coding-task trajectories or compact summaries retrieved for related tasks.
framework_agente: Claude Code based programming agent plus memory/retrieval variants
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-ContextBench: 1,100 base tasks and 376 related tasks from 51 repositories and 9 languages; SWE-ContextBench Lite: 300 base and 99 related Python tasks.
metricas_desempenho: Patch applicability, FAIL_TO_PASS tests/tasks, PASS_TO_PASS tests/tasks, resolved rate, runtime, estimated API cost.
metricas_cl: Context reuse accuracy, time efficiency, and cost efficiency across related coding tasks.
baseline_comparado: No-Experience baseline, Free Experience Reuse, Oracle Experience Reuse, Free Summary Reuse, Oracle Summary Reuse, Supermemory, OpenViking, mem0.
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: NA
custo_por_tarefa: No-Experience $0.79; Oracle Experience $0.77; Oracle Summary $0.85; Supermemory $0.58; OpenViking $0.53; mem0 $0.62 on 99 Lite related tasks.
reducao_tokens: NR exact token reduction; reports cost and runtime differences.
resultado_principal: SWE-ContextBench explicitly measures whether programming agents can accumulate, retrieve, and reuse prior context. Oracle Summary Reuse improves resolution to 34.34% versus 26.26% baseline; Supermemory reaches 30.30% resolved and OpenViking 29.20% with lower runtime/cost.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Oracle Summary Reuse: 34.34% resolved vs 26.26% No-Experience (+8.08pp). Supermemory: 30.30%; OpenViking: 29.20%; mem0: 24.24%.
custo_vs_baseline: OpenViking reduces average runtime/cost to 4.20 min/$0.53 vs No-Experience 6.37 min/$0.79; Supermemory 5.04 min/$0.58.
limitacoes: Lite experiments focus on 99 related Python tasks; autonomous context selection can be noisy; benchmark is preprint-stage.
ameacas_validade: Related-task construction, dependency setup, retrieval quality, and use of one Claude Code-based agent may affect external validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes TextBFGS maintaining and retaining a dynamic case base of historical error-to-operator correction trajectories for code optimization, with HumanEval and MBPP evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 013; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Zizheng Zhang, Yuyang Liao, Chen Chen, Jian He, Dun Wu, Qianjin Yu, Yanqin Gao, Jin Yang, Kailai Zhang, Eng Siong Chng, Xionghu Zhong
titulo: TextBFGS: A Case-Based Reasoning Approach to Code Optimization via Error-Operator Retrieval
formato_armazenamento: Dynamic Case Base of historical code-optimization trajectories stored in ChromaDB, with each case containing pre-optimization text, error feedback or gradient, abstract operator, and post-optimization text.
framework_agente: TextBFGS case-based reasoning optimizer with error-operator retrieval, one-pass update, and successful-case retention
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; in-domain and cross-domain case-base comparison; retrieval ablation; efficiency comparison
benchmarks_utilizados: HumanEval-Hard and MBPP-Hard filtered coding-optimization tasks
metricas_desempenho: Base Pass, Plus Pass, calls per task, tokens per call, tokens per task
metricas_cl: Case-base construction with 39 HumanEval trajectories and 245 MBPP trajectories; retrieval by error/gradient similarity; successful adaptations retained back into the case base.
baseline_comparado: TextGrad, TextGrad-Momentum, TextBFGS without case base, and TextBFGS-REMO with problem-based retrieval
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Case bases contain 39 HumanEval case trajectories and 245 MBPP trajectories in the reported setup; successful adaptations can be retained dynamically.
custo_por_tarefa: NR monetary cost; calls and token counts per task are reported.
reducao_tokens: HumanEval-Hard cross-domain TextBFGS uses 21.6k tokens/task versus 30.9k for TextGrad and 43.7k for TextGrad-Momentum; MBPP in-domain TextBFGS uses 17.2k tokens/task versus 26.6k for TextGrad.
resultado_principal: On HumanEval-Hard using the MBPP case base, TextBFGS reaches 97.78 Base Pass and 93.33 Plus Pass, exceeding TextBFGS without case base and REMO. On MBPP-Hard using the MBPP case base, TextBFGS reaches 94.02 Base Pass and 74.36 Plus Pass.
desempenho_vs_baseline: On HumanEval-Hard, TextBFGS reaches 97.78/93.33 versus 91.11/82.22 for TextBFGS without case base and 95.56/91.11 for REMO. Cross-domain TextBFGS maintains 74.36 MBPP Plus Pass using a HumanEval case base versus 58.12 for REMO.
custo_vs_baseline: Compared with TextGrad-Momentum on HumanEval-Hard, TextBFGS uses about 50.6% fewer tokens per task; the paper also reports lower calls per task than TextGrad/TextGrad-Momentum.
limitacoes: Evaluation focuses on filtered coding-optimization benchmarks and case trajectories from HumanEval/MBPP; generalization to larger repositories and richer optimization objectives is not established.
ameacas_validade: Filtered hard-task construction, case-base source domain, retrieval embedding design, baseline reimplementation choices, and maximum-iteration limits may affect validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes AiScientist maintaining durable workspace state through File-as-Bus artifacts so engineering agents can resume from persisted analyses, plans, code, and experimental evidence across long-horizon ML engineering loops, with PaperBench and MLE-Bench Lite evaluation.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 013; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Guoxin Chen, Jie Chen, Lei Chen, Jiale Zhao, Fanzhe Meng, Wayne Xin Zhao, Ruihua Song, Cheng Chen, Ji-Rong Wen, Kai Jia
titulo: Toward Autonomous Long-Horizon Engineering for ML Research
formato_armazenamento: Permission-scoped File-as-Bus workspace containing paper_analysis artifacts, submission artifacts, prioritized_task.md, plan.md, implementation logs, experiment logs, code files, run outputs, and concise summaries that agents can repeatedly re-ground on.
framework_agente: AiScientist hierarchical ML-research engineering system with Orchestrator, Paper Analyzer, Planning Specialist, Implementation Specialist, Experiment Specialist, and shared File-as-Bus workspace
llm_utilizado: Gemini-3-Flash and GLM-5 in main evaluations; GPT-5.4 grader for PaperBench scoring
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; long-horizon task evaluation; ablation; cost comparison
benchmarks_utilizados: PaperBench and MLE-Bench Lite
metricas_desempenho: PaperBench score, MLE-Bench Lite any-medal rate, cost per task, ablation score drops
metricas_cl: Durable artifact-mediated continuity through File-as-Bus workspace, re-grounding of specialized agents, workspace-map summarization, and preserved implementation/experiment evidence.
baseline_comparado: BasicAgent, IterAgent, AiScientist without File-as-Bus, and MLE-Bench Lite leaderboard/baseline systems
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Workspace accumulates analyses, plans, code, logs, and experimental artifacts across each long-horizon task; no aggregate memory-size curve is reported.
custo_por_tarefa: PaperBench cost/task is reported: Gemini AiScientist $15.67 versus IterAgent $27.44; GLM-5 AiScientist $12.20 versus IterAgent $54.90. Full 20-task PaperBench grading costs about $832.
resultado_principal: AiScientist improves PaperBench scores from 20.60 to 30.52 with Gemini and from 22.37 to 33.73 with GLM-5. On MLE-Bench Lite, AiScientist reports 81.82% any-medal rate with both Gemini-3-Flash and GLM-5.
desempenho_vs_baseline: On PaperBench, AiScientist improves over IterAgent by 9.92 points with Gemini and 11.36 points with GLM-5. Removing File-as-Bus drops PaperBench by 6.41 points and MLE-Bench Lite any-medal rate by 31.82 points.
custo_vs_baseline: AiScientist is cheaper than IterAgent in the reported PaperBench setup: $15.67 versus $27.44 per task with Gemini and $12.20 versus $54.90 with GLM-5.
limitacoes: Evaluation is expensive and constrained to benchmarked ML research engineering tasks; performance depends on specialized agent roles, workspace conventions, and available compute/time budgets.
ameacas_validade: Benchmark selection, 24-hour task limit, H20 GPU environment, grading model, selected backbones, workspace permissions, and comparison to simplified agents may affect validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes Traversal-as-Policy distilling OpenHands execution logs into a persistent executable Gated Behavior Tree with experience-grounded gate updates, spine memory, and SWE-bench/OpenHands evaluation.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `li2026traversalaspolicy` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_skill_rule_library. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Peiran Li, Jiashuo Sun, Fangzhou Lin, Shuo Xing, Tianfu Fu, Suofei Feng, Chaoqun Ni, Zhengzhong Tu
titulo: Traversal-as-Policy: Log-Distilled Gated Behavior Trees as Externalized, Verifiable Policies for Safe, Robust, and Efficient Agents
formato_armazenamento: Gated Behavior Tree (GBT): árvore executável single-rooted com 21 task-family roots. Cada nó macro = (description NL, coarse tags, risk_level, behavior_signature σ = (σ_disc, σ_cont), env_context pointer). Gates determinísticos: RuleGates (code predicates sobre ctx estruturado) + ContentGates (frozen Llama-Guard-3-8B classifier, temp=0). Spine memory = sequência compacta de macros visitados como estado de longo prazo. Self-evolution (GBT-SE): repair local via analogical successes com regression testing preservando safety invariants.
framework_agente: openhands
llm_utilizado: GPT-4o (primary base model), Llama-3-8B, Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking (executor decoupling); Llama-Guard-3-8B (ContentGate classifier)
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified (500), WebArena (812), GPQA (448), AgentSafetyBench, AgentHarm (public), Agent Security Bench (ASB)
metricas_desempenho: Success Rate (SR%), Coverage (Cov%), Violation Rate (Viol%), Unsafe Success (USucc%), Tok/Chars (thousands), AgentSafetyBench score, HarmScore, ASB metrics
baseline_comparado: OpenHands CodeActAgent (gpt-4o native), +Global guardrail only, +GBT-Basic, +GBT-SE (ablation ladder progressiva)
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: GBT cresce via self-evolution (novos children, gates); spine memory é compacta per-episode (visitados macros apenas); tree é artefato persistente cross-benchmark
custo_por_tarefa: NR
reducao_tokens: -39% Tok (208k→126k) e -40% Chars (820k→490k) no SWE-bench Verified; -45% Tok (94k→52k) e -43% Chars (360k→205k) no WebArena
resultado_principal: GBT-SE eleva success rate de 34.6% para 73.6% no SWE-bench Verified e de 19.7% para 66.9% no WebArena, enquanto reduz violations para 0.2% e unsafe success para 0.0%. Tokens reduzidos ~40%. Com 8B executors (Qwen3-VL-8B), GBT mais que triplica success no SWE-bench (14.0%→58.8%), demonstrando decoupling entre reasoning capacity offline e execução online.
desempenho_vs_baseline: +39.0pp SWE-bench (73.6% vs 34.6%); +47.2pp WebArena (66.9% vs 19.7%); +28.5pp GPQA (87.3% vs 58.7%); violations 2.8%→0.2% SWE-bench; unsafe success 1.2%→0.0%
custo_vs_baseline: Tok/Chars 208k/820k→126k/490k SWE-bench (-39%/-40%); 94k/360k→52k/205k WebArena (-45%/-43%); 8B executors viáveis com same GBT artifact
limitacoes: Coverage boundary explícito: fora de coverage (covered=0), GBT só garante primitive-level safety; distillation de logs pode herdar vieses dos trajectórios OpenHands; 21 task families fixas podem não cobrir todos domínios; gates dependem de structured ctx que pode omitir estado relevante
ameacas_validade: GPT-4o como base model primário; GBT distilado de logs OpenHands pode não transferir para outros frameworks; safety gates determinísticos sobre ctx podem falhar se ctx omitir campos relevantes; avaliação Protocol A (cross-benchmark distillation) pode ter overlap semântico residual; macro abstraction stability testada mas não garantida para todos cenários

full_text_rationale: Full text introduces a long-horizon coding-agent benchmark for emergent specification tracking and evaluates ProjectGuard, an external project-state layer that persists semantic design commitments and structural codebase state across coding turns and proactive restarts, with quantitative Claude Code and Codex results.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 013; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Lu Yan, Xuan Chen, Xiangyu Zhang
titulo: When the Specification Emerges: Benchmarking Faithfulness Loss in Long-Horizon Coding Agents
formato_armazenamento: ProjectGuard stores semantic project state with revisable design commitments and structural project state with files, classes, functions, and interfaces, then uses compatibility-aware forecasting and proactive restart decisions across coding turns.
framework_agente: ProjectGuard external project-state layer evaluated within the SLUMP emergent-specification benchmark for Claude Code and Codex
llm_utilizado: Claude Code and Codex coding platforms
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; emergent-versus-single-shot comparison; mitigation case study; platform comparison
benchmarks_utilizados: SLUMP benchmark built from 20 ML papers, 371 components, and approximately 60 coding requests per paper
metricas_desempenho: Mean Component Faithfulness, DIR, IF50, fully faithful component counts, severe failures, compaction/restart behavior, state size
metricas_cl: External persistent semantic and structural project state across long-horizon coding turns, compatibility forecasts, and proactive restarts.
baseline_comparado: Emergent specification runs, single-shot specification control, and ProjectGuard mitigation on Claude Code/Codex
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Mean external state size is reported as 390 KB for Claude Code and 286 KB for Codex in ProjectGuard runs.
custo_por_tarefa: NR monetary cost; external state size and compaction/restart behavior are reported.
resultado_principal: Under emergent specification, Claude Code drops from 3.031 MCF single-shot to 2.718 emergent and Codex drops from 3.245 to 3.242; ProjectGuard on Claude raises MCF from 2.718 to 3.000, recovers 90% of the gap, increases fully faithful components from 118 to 181, and reduces severe failures from 72 to 49.
desempenho_vs_baseline: Single-shot outperforms emergent specification on 16 of 20 papers for Claude Code and 14 of 20 for Codex. ProjectGuard improves Claude IF50 on 15 of 20 papers; Codex semantic gains are small but DIR improves on 15 of 19 analyzed cases.
custo_vs_baseline: ProjectGuard adds external state averaging hundreds of KB and changes compaction/restart behavior; no monetary or token-cost comparison is reported.
limitacoes: ProjectGuard is evaluated as a mitigation case study for emergent specification, not as a complete standalone coding-agent architecture across many software-engineering domains.
ameacas_validade: Component scoring rubric, paper selection, emergent request scripts, Claude Code/Codex platform behavior, hidden artifact design, and ProjectGuard implementation choices may affect validity.

full_text_rationale: Full text describes MemCoder structuring historical human coding experience into long-term structured memory, retrieving it for later SWE-bench issue resolution, and crystallizing validated solutions into persistent memory.
extractor_notes: Exact-title prior extraction `deng2026memcoder` reused after search text/title/path check; architecture_status=arch_repository_memory. Controlled-comparison status remains prior-prefilled pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Yi-Xuan Deng, Xiaoqin Liu, Yi Zhang, Guo-Wei Yang, Shuojin Yang
titulo: Your Code Agent Can Grow Alongside You with Structured Memory
formato_armazenamento: Vector database (FAISS) de memory entries estruturados como sextuplas: (commit message o, code changes c, functional keywords k, problem description p, root-cause analysis r, summarized solution s). Construídos de commits históricos via LLM polisher (Defect Management theory). Novas entradas adicionadas via self-internalization de soluções human-verified.
framework_agente: openhands
benchmarks_utilizados: SWE-bench Verified (500)
metricas_desempenho: Resolved rate (%), number of resolved issues
baseline_comparado: OpenHands (Claude Opus 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Pro), TRAE, DaubanCode, Live-SWE-agent, EPAM AI/Run, Atlassian Rover, ACoder, Warp
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: Cresce monotonicamente com commits do repositório + soluções internalizadas; top-k retrieval controla exposição
custo_por_tarefa: NR
resultado_principal: MemCoder atinge 78.8% (pass@1) e 83.8% (pass@2) com GPT-5.2 no SWE-bench Verified, comparável ao SOTA. Com DeepSeek-V3.2, alcança 77.8% vs 68.4% sem memória (+9.4pp), demonstrando que memória estruturada de commits desbloqueia modelos gerais para SE complexa.
desempenho_vs_baseline: +9.4pp vs sem memória com DeepSeek-V3.2 (77.8% vs 68.4%); +3.4pp vs OpenHands+GPT-5.2 (78.8% vs ~74.4%); top-6 no leaderboard SWE-bench Verified
custo_vs_baseline: NR
limitacoes: Avaliação apenas no SWE-bench Verified; depende de histórico de commits rico; qualidade da memória limitada pela capacidade do LLM de destilação; overhead de retrieval não quantificado; ablação em subset de 200 instâncias
ameacas_validade: SWE-bench Verified como único benchmark; DeepSeek-V3.2 e GPT-5.2 como únicos modelos; memória construída de commits do mesmo repositório avaliado (temporal leakage controlada mas residual); cross-encoder reranker pode introduzir latência não reportada

full_text_rationale: Full text describes SARE as a research-coding agent with a persistent global training context and cumulative cheatsheet that stores reusable strategies across tasks, evaluated on SUPER, ResearchCodeBench, and ScienceAgentBench.
extractor_notes: Fresh search architecture extraction batch 013; controlled-comparison and quality status remain pending aggregate recomputation.
autores: Balaji Dinesh Gangireddi, Aniketh Garikaparthi, Manasi Patwardhan, Arman Cohan
titulo: Self-Adapting Agents for Automating Research Coding Workflows
formato_armazenamento: Global Training Context with auxiliary context, reflection history, and cumulative cheatsheet storing recurring strategies, common fixes, domain-specific knowledge, failure modes, and reusable procedural heuristics across research-coding tasks.
framework_agente: SARE Self-Adapting Research Engineer with Actor, Reflector, Global Training Context, code-based prompt/cheatsheet editing, and cross-repository adaptation
metodo_avaliacao: benchmark; offline and online adaptation; ablation; cost table
benchmarks_utilizados: SUPER, ResearchCodeBench, ScienceAgentBench
metricas_desempenho: Submission rate, landmarks, output match, overall score, ResearchCodeBench accuracy, ScienceAgentBench success rate, CodeBLEU
metricas_cl: Offline and online adaptation with training/validation/test splits, persistent Global Training Context, cumulative cheatsheet growth, reflection history, and ablations removing cheatsheet, auxiliary context, reflection, or the whole Global Training Context.
baseline_comparado: Baseline agent, Static SOTA, GEPA-style adaptation, SARE without ground truth, SARE with ground truth, and SARE ablations without cheatsheet, auxiliary context, reflection, or Global Training Context
tamanho_memoria_ao_longo_tempo: The cheatsheet and Global Training Context grow during adaptation, but the paper does not report total memory size over time.
custo_por_tarefa: SUPER costs about $1-3 per task; ResearchCodeBench and ScienceAgentBench are reported at about $1 per task in the appendix cost table.
resultado_principal: With ground truth in offline adaptation, SARE reaches 88.9 submission, 38.1 landmarks, 31.4 output match, and 52.8 overall on SUPER, plus 35.41 ResearchCodeBench accuracy and 30.6 ScienceAgentBench success rate.
desempenho_vs_baseline: SARE without ground truth improves the SUPER overall score to 38.8 versus 29.9 for the baseline, while SARE with ground truth reaches 52.8. Ablating the whole Global Training Context drops SUPER overall from 34.7 to 16.0 in the reported ablation table.
custo_vs_baseline: Per-task benchmark costs are reported at roughly $1-3, but the paper does not isolate the additional cost of reflection and context growth versus baselines.
limitacoes: Reflective calls and growing Global Training Context introduce overhead and can approach context-window limits; evaluation is concentrated on research-coding benchmarks and selected baselines.
ameacas_validade: Benchmark representativeness, train/validation/test split choices, use of ground-truth traces in offline adaptation, single LLM stack, anonymous artifact status, and comparison-baseline implementation choices may affect validity.
